Increasing participation in the length and the breadth of our education system was something that the Hawke government achieved. There were big increases in the retention rates for school students to year 12—an important development, because that then meant they were able to access our higher education system. There were important reforms to the structure of the higher education system that led to a significant increase in participation. There was also the introduction of an income-contingent student loan scheme—HECS—which once again became transformational for our higher education system. Many will also be familiar with another memorable achievement that he had in higher education: sculling a yard of ale—two and a half pints, or 1.4 litres—in 11 seconds, a then world record at Oxford.

While at Oxford, Hawke wrote his thesis on the history of wage fixing in Australia for his Bachelor of Letters degree. It was entitled 'An appraisal of the role of the Australian Commonwealth Court of Conciliation and Arbitration with special reference to the development of the concept of a basic wage'. In her memoirs, Hazel Hawke recounts Bob returning from this final oral exam on his research for his thesis. When asked how it had gone, he replied, 'Those Pommy blokes'—and I'm sure he used another phrase—'told me I knew more about it than they did.'

Hawke's widow, Blanche d'Alpuget, listed the large increase in the proportion of children finishing high school as one of Hawke's proudest achievements. Between 1985 and 1992, the retention of school students to year 12 increased from 46 per cent to 77 per cent. In 1988, the year before the Dawkins reforms were implemented, almost 169,000 students commenced higher education in Australia. By the end of 1996, this had increased to over 261,000—growth of almost 55 per cent. By contrast, Australia's population had grown by about 10 per cent in the same period. It was another remarkable achievement. Prior to those reforms, Australia had a binary system of higher education, with universities and with colleges of advanced education that were often quite small and that focused primarily on teaching.

The Dawkins reforms saw the end of this two-tier system, with the amalgamation of the colleges of advanced education, either with each other or with existing universities, to form one unified national system. Between 1983 and 1996, 18 universities were established. That's a near-doubling in the number of 19 universities that existed when Hawke came to power in 1983, and, as we in this space all know, being able to access higher education can have life-changing impacts. There is no question that the broadening of our university sector brought education closer to home for many Australians. Many of those universities have gone on to great things and contribute not only to the education of Australians but to one of our nation's largest exports, our $35 billion international education sector.

With such growth, of course, came the need to ensure that higher education in Australia was sustainable, and the introduction by the Hawke government of the HECS system, with which many of us are familiar, in 1989 laid the groundwork for long-term sustainability. It's incredibly important to note that countries in Latin America and, to our north, in South-East Asia are now looking to introduce schemes of exactly this type so they can broaden participation in higher education. That is the true mark of long-lasting reform.

Many of us who were alive and studying in 1989 will remember the tragic loss of life at Tiananmen Square at the time and Hawke's decision to allow Chinese nationals who were studying in Australia to remain here. At that time, there were more than 10,000 Chinese nationals studying in Australia. Another important legacy as a result of that compassionate decision is that currently there are 150,000 Chinese nationals studying in Australia today. I have no doubt that the decision taken after Tiananmen Square in 1989 has led to the continued use by Chinese nationals of our higher education system. It is wonderful that the government has been able to announce today the scholarship program that will bear Bob Hawke's name. There is no question that education was one of the things that he was incredibly proud of, not only of his own but of what he was able to do to increase Australians' participation in our education system. It is a legacy that is not only still being felt in Australia today but across the world.

Can I take this moment to pass on my sympathies to Bob Hawke's family. He obviously had a life and a family that had its trials, tribulations and joys like all Australian families. But there is no question that we have lost a great Australian. I wish to pass on my sympathies and my condolences to the Hawke family.

It is a privilege to participate in this motion of condolence. In starting, I thank the government for allowing the extent of this debate to occur in this chamber—the chamber in which the first Prime Minister to speak was Robert James Lee Hawke. I also thank the Prime Minister for establishing the award in Mr Hawke's name to be given to students of excellence, and I think that is certainly fitting of the memory of Bob Hawke.

Much has been said of Bob Hawke so far and much more is yet to be said, but he certainly was a man who believed in Australia, and I think it's fair to say that Australia believed in him. He was a true son of the labour movement, but I believe, beyond that, he was probably Australia's greatest agent of change. There is no doubt that our nation will be forever in his debt, I think, for the magnitude of change that occurred under his leadership as such, not only the structural reform of our economy but also our outlook as a nation.

As a young union official at the time, I was in awe of Bob Hawke's passion and determination that championed the lives of working men and women. I remember how he inspired us in his capacity as President of the Australian Council of Trade Unions. Even then, as President of the ACTU, he was a household name—he was as well known as any public official in the land at that stage.

I remember him being at the forefront of major industrial disputes. He was a skilled negotiator, a fantastic advocate in the Industrial Relations Commission. But, above all, what I remember about him in those days is the way he sought consensus. He was universally respected—whether it was employers, employee representatives, government or elsewhere, this man commanded their respect.

My earliest recollection of him was his ability to persuade strong and powerful unions to moderate their industrial positions to ensure that workers in some of the weaker sectors of our economy were not left behind. No doubt that had some bearing on his thinking after he came to government in 1983 about the prices and income accord—the idea of a social wage. That was certainly born out of that, but I saw that in evidence when he was leading the ACTU. He genuinely cared for all workers.

Most of us know him as Australia's longest-serving Labor Prime Minister. He will be forever remembered for his work in transforming the framework, particularly the economic framework, of this country. He and Paul Keating were such a formidable duo. It wasn't that they did this by strength; they did it by encouraging people with the argument that we can be better. He deregulated the financial sector. He floated the Australian dollar. He opened up our economy to the world. He caused us to be a more confident nation and certainly a more outward looking country.

However, it's not just these great achievements in the economy that he will be remembered for. He was also an avid believer in the environment and he knew wholeheartedly the importance of increasing the government's focus on environment protection. To this end, he pushed for the protection of Antarctica. He led a UN task force at a time when international interests were keen to have mining of some description in Antarctica, and sympathetic states such as the UK and the US, whilst they didn't actually have notions of banning mining, certainly thought there was scope to have regulated mining in Antarctica. Hawke sought international cooperation. It wasn't that he opposed mining there—he went one step beyond. He wanted to get a blanket prohibition on mining in the Antarctic, and, after securing, first, the support of the French, he then systematically found allies in other international arenas and he established exactly that. His effort in Antarctica not only prevented mining; it brought the focus of scientific research, which is now so critical to our understanding of climate change itself.

He also fought on many other fronts: protection of our natural environment here at home, the prevention of the damming of the Franklin river, the expansion of protection for the Lemonthyme and Southern Forests in Tasmania, and the Daintree rainforest in Queensland; and—the one that Warren Snowdon spoke about a little earlier—the banning of uranium mining in Jabiluka and Kakadu's Coronation Hill. He pursued these on the basis that he saw their World Heritage value and these parts of our environment deserved to be protected. He didn't do it for political expediency. He didn't do this to get a vote. He did this in the genuine belief that, for future generations, the natural beauty of our country needed to be preserved. In his own words, he said this:

The essence of power is the knowledge that what you do is going to have an effect, not just an immediate but perhaps a lifelong effect, on the happiness and wellbeing of millions of people …

We just heard the Minister for Education speak, and I thank him for the words he said. Bob Hawke's investment in education and his investment in universal healthcare are a stark reminder that he worked not only for Australia; he worked for every Australian. By the way, at the start of Bob Hawke's prime ministership in 1983, only three out of every 10 kids completed high school—three out of 10. By the time he left the prime ministership, it was eight out of 10. He set us on the path of becoming a smarter nation. The benefits that flowed from that were improvements in tertiary education, the HECS scheme that followed et cetera. Really, getting more young Australians to finish high school did materially affect where we are now. He really set out to create a smarter nation.

Through his more persuasive efforts, he certainly convinced significant elements of the trade union movement in this country that looking after workers wasn't just about awards and rates of pay but also included looking after the dignity of people in their retirement. In 1983, when he became Prime Minister, very few blue-collar workers enjoyed retirement benefits. Through his efforts we saw the birth of universal superannuation, one of the greatest advances for workers in generations.

He had an unshakable belief in our nation's potential. He thought that we could be a better nation, and in his government he set about achieving that. He had a very down-to-earth manner about him. He was a genuine personality, and Australians followed him over the eight years of his prime ministership.

One of the other things that I do remember vividly is how moved he was and how affected he was following the massacre at Tiananmen Square. He felt that so personally, and it was right that, following Tiananmen Square, he let thousands of Chinese students who were studying remain in this country. It also showed his great depth of humanitarian spirit. But for me—and the member for McMahon at the table, the member for Werriwa and those of us who have the honour of representing some of the most multicultural communities in this country—the efforts of Bob Hawke also led to our nation becoming a more inclusive and open country. We are now certainly the net beneficiaries of the extent of immigration that has occurred in our land. But we have also seen the great depth of humanitarian spirit that did flow from those efforts, particularly as a result of the massacre in Tiananmen Square.

Before I leave his humanitarian aspects, what I think has also got to be seen as an enduring legacy of Bob Hawke is his work to end apartheid in South Africa and his efforts to free Nelson Mandela. It was his selfless and forward thinking that really reinvigorated the Australian spirit—namely, we can make a difference for the better in our world.

Following our defeat in 2013 and as we moved into 2014, as the Chief Opposition Whip, I thought that it would be beneficial if we had a function where we got caucus members—and also, importantly, their staff—together. It wasn't to put bandaids on things, but I think we did need to reinvigorate our party at that stage. And we thought: who would we get who would inspire the notion of Labor and get us back fighting for reform, fighting for change—those things that Labor's best known for? We actually got Bob Hawke to come, and it wasn't difficult. He asked me, 'What do you want me to do?' and I said: 'If you could, just speak to the dinner for maybe 10 to 15 minutes. Just give people a bit of a rev-up. I know we've had an election loss, so you could actually put a bit of spirit back into our side.' He said, 'Ten to 15 minutes?' 'Yes, Bob, that'd be marvellous if you could do that for us.'

Fifty-five minutes later—meanwhile, the entrees and everything else were getting cold—everyone was still spellbound by this bloke who had just captivated us. He had taken us through every major Labor initiative—social reforms whose making you could be thrilled that we had had our fingerprints on. He said, 'This is where we need to go for the future.' And then he actually broke into the song 'Solidarity Forever'. Most of us could at least hum along with the chorus line, but Bob knew every verse of it. As a matter of fact, he sang every verse with the microphone—I don't think he had it at the time, but someone gave it to him, so he walked around the room doing this. He had such a great night. Jill Saunders, who was Bob's PA and who worked with him in Parliament House and who was working with him as a former Prime Minister—this was getting late in the night—said to me: 'Look, I've got to go. I'm going back to my motel. He's now your responsibility.' I didn't quite realise the extent of that.

We were sitting the next day, so everyone was peeling off and going. It got down to where there were only about three or four of us left. One of them was me, of course, because I had the responsibility to get him back to where we had to go. There was Bob and my wife, Bernadette, who was trying to encourage him out because they were turning the lights off in the Press Club at that stage, until one of the young waitresses who was serving the tables actually came out. One of her friends—I forget what her name was—actually said, 'Mr Hawke, it's her birthday today; she's turning 23.' That changed everything. He had this young woman come over. He put his arm around her. Then he sang to her, and this went on and on. By the way, we had to have another drink while all of this was occurring, of course. When we left, the lights were going out. There were only about three of us left walking out of the Press Club. Fortunately, I had a car waiting that only had to go 100 metres up the road to Hotel Realm. I got him up there and got him to his room. I thought Jill Saunders did well by actually handing him over that night, but he had such a great night. He said, 'This is what it is to be Labor: to go out and be able to enthuse the Labor spirit—having enthused people who are committed to go on to make change.'

A couple of weeks before his death, I got a call from Craig Emerson. He said that Bob's been having a few discussions with Blanche of what might happen after. I said, 'Oh, okay.' As long as I've known Bob, despite the fact that his father was a religious minister, I don't think Bob really thought there was another God head other than Bob—that's probably putting it inappropriately. Anyway, I thought he was a lifelong committed atheist. Craig suggested to me that he'd just like to have a talk to someone and asked if I knew anyone who could go and sit with him and have a talk. I said, 'I'm sure we can find somebody.' But he said: 'There are a few things. It's got to be someone who has some Labor leaning.' I said, 'Okay, we can do that.' And then he said, 'But someone who is sympathetic to trade unions.' So that narrowed the field a little. Anyway, I had a talk to Bishop Terry Brady in Sydney. I had known that Bishop Brady's father was an official in the Building Workers' Industrial Union. I was told by Craig that Blanche had agreed that it sounds like I had the right bloke, so Terry went over to see Bob on a very warm Autumn afternoon. Terry sat in the sun while Bob spoke to him. I think Bob probably did most of the speaking in between his large cigar. Bishop Terry told me, 'I sort of sat downwind of this cigar, soaking in the aroma.' He said, 'I'm not sure who got the most out of all of that.' Bishop Terry told me, 'This is a person who I've met today who has so fundamentally changed this nation for the better.' I'm not sure where their spiritual discussion went, but I certainly know that Bishop Terry got a heck of a lot out of his meeting that afternoon with Bob.

It is a privilege to have been a part of the labour movement. I worked with Bob Hawke through his time at the ACTU and I've seen what he achieved in this place when he was able to continue to care for Australian working families in such a material way by making the changes that have enabled what we currently have. He has put us on the path for advancement. He has changed the way we think about ourselves. This man has made a difference for the better in our country. To Blanche and to his children I offer my sincere condolences. To Bob Hawke: may you rest in peace.

Today, we have the great privilege of honouring the memory and legacy—and to commemorate and celebrate the life—of Robert James Lee Hawke. Of course, many people will know him as the ACTU president, the member for Wills and Labor's most successful Prime Minister. But I should reflect that he was also a long-time resident of the federal electorate of Goldstein, living for many years on Royal Avenue in Sandringham. After his death, because of the affection for him held by many Australians, there were little tributes set out the front of his former home in Royal Avenue in Sandringham. People dropped off flowers and set up candles. This was many years after he had left our community and gone not just to the Lodge and Kirribilli but also to live in Sydney as well.

I will say though that, throughout his entire time while he was the member for Wills and Prime Minister and lived in the federal electorate of Goldstein, despite the spirit of him always wishing solidarity forever, the federal electorate of Balaclava and then Goldstein never sent a federal Labor member. I'm sure it had nothing to do with his personality or the great sense of respect that the residents had but was a reflection merely of political values that the community shared.

The Prime Minister this morning referred to the fact that Bob Hawke was the Prime Minister who saw over the end of one era in Australia's political life and the beginning of a new. When I was studying at university, it was often a common thing that we studied books like The End of Certainty, that covered much of his prime ministership and, of course, his achievements. While there are many things that people can say about Bob Hawke, the thing that I always respected about Bob Hawke, even though I had different political leanings, or at least partisan leanings, was that he understood the importance of having justice in a free society. In his first speech he said:

… privilege without concern, without compassion and without a real contribution to others less privileged is not only an obscenity but also will ultimately be self-destructive.

That principle that he held true to is right, and it continues to inform so much of what he would call us to do today: to continue to make sure that we build an Australia that takes everybody forward together.

Many people in this place have already referred to the fact that when his mother was bearing him the Bible fell over and came to the book of Isaiah, and she made remarks about how the newborn child would have the weight of government on his shoulders. But the brutal reality is that there's no point having power if you don't know what to do with it. The weight of government is one not just of administration but of leadership. And what Bob Hawke did—and many people have cited examples already today—was take incredible stands that helped modernise Australia at the time. Obviously, there were significant achievements in the economic space, particularly around floating the dollar and exposing Australia to global competition and the establishment of APEC and the contribution it made to the development of international organisations to promote free trade and peace throughout the world. There was the contribution he made to make sure that Antarctica was mine-free, and he made a commitment to the stewardship of the environment that we should want for our country and our world into the future. But one of his most remarkable—and, frankly, boldest—achievements was, of course, his preparedness after the Tiananmen Square massacre to say to those Chinese students who were in Australia that they should be able to stay.

Hawke's legacy is, of course, of policy. Yes, there's a part of it that's around the narrative of being a larrikin. There is, of course, the story of his rise and fall. But I think his legacy for us, to inspire us today, is to have courage about what Australia can achieve. Regardless of our partisan leanings, I think that's what he embodied through his honesty, his character and what he brought to Old Parliament House and this Parliament House as well. We should be encouraged and inspired by his leadership and his contribution to our nation. May he rest in peace.

My first political memory—in fact, one of my clearest early memories—was 5 March 1983, the day the Australian people elected the Hawke Labor government. I remember my parents quietly going to Smithfield Public School, where I attended, to cast their votes for Bob Hawke to become Prime Minister—or, more accurately, for my predecessor, Dr Dick Klugman, to be the member for Prospect, as our seat was then called. He was also, ironically enough, my predecessor as shadow health minister. My parents and those millions of Australians who voted to elect Bob Hawke did so with hope, but they could not have envisaged the sea change, the massive revolution, nor the significance of the government that they were electing on 5 March 1983.

I didn't see it at the time, but I've said to people since that if you want to understand the significance, the star that Bob Hawke was in the Australian political landscape, use the wonders of the internet that we now have and YouTube the arrival of Bob Hawke at the National Tally Room down the road here in Canberra; see the aura he had as he and Hazel entered the National Tally Room as the Prime Minister-elect of Australia. He said not a word. He didn't give a speech. He simply walked from one end of the tally room to the other. The impact that had, as the whole country stopped to watch, was enormous. He then undertook a press conference where he thanked the Australian people for their confidence and sent a message to those Australians who did not vote for him. Specifically, he said that he wanted to address the people of Tasmania, who voted very solidly against the Labor Party in that election and elected not one Labor MP, to say that he wanted to lead a government for all Australians—and he certainly did that.

He did not see his personal popularity—which, of course, was enormous, with an approval rating of more than 70 per cent—as something to be kept in a cupboard and not put to work for the Australian people. He used that popularity, that political capital, as a bulwark to lead the most reformist government in Australia's history, to lead the most significant and sustained period of economic and social change in our country. As has been said, he led a cabinet of talents in a partnership with the Treasurer, Paul Keating, with the most enormous consequences: to modernise the country in a way which had not been attempted or achieved in the past. Much has been made of his reforms—rightly so—of opening the economy, floating the dollar, tearing down the tariff walls, engaging in the great productivity and competitiveness revolution, and his national competition policy. Much has been rightly made of financial deregulation, opening up to foreign banks, freeing the capital markets, liberalising the labour market and ensuring that the economy was modernised and open.

He and his government knew that the whole purpose of that was to give something back. They introduced the best healthcare system in the world, Medicare, against ferocious opposition from the medical establishment and from honourable members opposite at that point. They were determined to do so in a way not only to implement but cement the reforms. They were building on the great achievement of Bill Hayden, who should be acknowledged in this debate, the social security minister of the Whitlam government, who had, against the same fierce opposition, introduced Medibank. They took that as a model and introduced Medicare, ensuring that they brought the Australian people with them so that the reform was cemented and so that, despite the political opposition, after successive Labor governments, it could never be undone.

Likewise, the beginnings of universal superannuation were completed in the Keating government. It gave Australians the chance of a dignified retirement. Superannuation had been the preserve of a privileged few until the establishment of the Hawke-Keating government and their reforms. They knew that Australians who worked hard all of their lives deserved a dignified retirement. Putting the miracle of compound interest to work for those millions of Australians was something that only a Labor government could achieve. Other honourable members have mentioned the lifting of school retention rates from around 30 per cent to over 80 per cent so that eight in 10 young Australians finished school and had the chance for better and further education. All of these social reforms, investing in the social fabric and providing social conditions and standards, would become the bulwark of our society and could not be resisted.

He engaged in economic reforms that were hugely controversial at the time. He introduced the capital gains tax and the fringe benefits tax, which honourable members opposite went to the 1986 election proposing to repeal. He knew that this broadening of the tax base was absolutely essential for the fairness of the tax system as well as for providing the financing of important investments in our social fabric. This was Hawke the reformer and the tackler of tough issues.

There was an arrangement between himself and his Treasurer. Hawke could use his very considerable communications skills, matched with those of his Treasurer, to convince the Australian people of the merits of their case. Much of it looks easy in hindsight—inevitable, almost undeniable. But that was not the case. It was hard-fought reform not only in this room but, at some points, in our party. Hawke and Keating famously lost an argument about privatisation at a national conference and delayed the privatisation of Qantas and Australian Airlines by two years, as they continued to argue for these reforms in the party. Of course, they inevitably held the day. This was Hawke the great reformer, for whom our country owes a great deal.

Like many honourable members, I came to know Bob Hawke a little later in life. I know that some honourable members know his great partner, and sometimes rival, Paul Keating much more closely. Paul Keating has always recognised the innate power of their partnership: they could only do what they did together, and they could only achieve what they did as a partnership. I last saw Bob at a function for Hannah Beazley, our candidate for the seat of Swan, in Sydney. I was conducting that for her. Being so close to her father, Governor Beazley, he wanted to come to lend his support. I could tell, at that point, that we were nearing the end for Bob Hawke, but we had a good talk on that occasion.

He maintained his interest in our movement, our party and the country right until the end. Honourable members will recall his joint stewardship, his joint op-ed in the Fairfax pages in the final stages of the election campaign, endorsing the Labor Party and our economic agenda in the recent election. That was something that Dr Emerson and I had been discussing with Mr Keating, as we talked about whether such an op-ed—it would be the first time they had come together in decades—were possible. They were both keen to do it. Such was Bob Hawke's keen interest. As has been noted, he didn't want to vote postally; he wanted to actually cast a vote on the Saturday for the Labor Party. He wanted to hold on that long. But, alas, it was not to be.

Bob Hawke has been rightly noted and lauded by the Prime Minister and others on the other side as one of Australia's greatest Prime Ministers, appropriately and rightly so—because he is. He will always be one of the country's great reformers. He was somebody who dreamed of what could be and somebody who knew it would not be easy but would tackle it nevertheless, because we were not living up to our potential as a country. We were falling short of our massive potential. Bob Hawke could see that in every day of his prime ministership. Bob Hawke was a great Australian in every sense of the word. He was a passionate, patriotic, loving Australian. He loved our country, and he was determined to deliver for it. He was a remarkable man, a remarkable politician. We shall not know his like again. I join with the House in extending my condolences for his loss but my celebration of a life very well lived.

I too am privileged to rise to speak on this condolence motion on the death of the Hon. Robert James Lee Hawke, the 23rd Prime Minister of Australia. Indeed, it's fitting as I'm one of only three members of this chamber—indeed three members of the parliament—who served when Bob Hawke was Prime Minister, the other two being the member for Lingiari, who spoke earlier on this motion, and my good friend the member for Monash. They both served at the time when Bob Hawke was Prime Minister. Indeed, he was Prime Minister when I was first elected to this place in May 1991. It was a position he held until the end of December 1991, when, as others have remarked upon, he was then toppled by Paul Keating and subsequently retired from parliament on 20 February 1992.

Bob Hawke was elected Prime Minister in 1983 and became Labor's longest-serving Prime Minister, and at that time the nation's second longest-serving Prime Minister, a length of time only eclipsed subsequently by John Howard. Bob Hawke was well-known to a generation or two, if not three, of Australians. Prior to coming here, he was a well-known figure in the Australian polity. His time as advocate of the ACTU, his presidency of the ACTU, his presidency of the Australian Labor Party all meant that Australians in every part of this country knew of Bob Hawke and what Bob Hawke stood for.

And on coming here and becoming Prime Minister he made an enormous contribution to the welfare and the wellbeing of Australians, as so many other honourable members have noted. I won't repeat all the remarkable achievements of the Hawke-Keating government: the floating of the dollar; the dismantling of the tariffs; reforming of taxation; the introduction of Medicare, which the previous speaker, the member for McMahon, spoke about; and, indeed, the reform of industrial relations. Many of these matters were supported by the then opposition, but it was indeed the leadership of Mr Hawke and his Treasurer at the time, Mr Keating, that led to remarkable changes in the Australian economy and, indeed, in the outlook of this country.

In his contribution earlier in the day, the member for Corio referred to the lineage of Jefferson, Lincoln and the Roosevelts in the United States of America. It reminded me of that book by Doris Kearns Goodwin, entitled Team of Rivals, which spoke about Abraham Lincoln's amazing presidency and life in the United States. The thing about that was that Lincoln presided over a very talented group of cabinet members, and the same can be said about Bob Hawke. It strikes me that if a Prime Minister, if a leader, doesn't always want to be the smartest person in the room but can recognise and can encourage the contributions, the talents and the expertise of those also sitting around the cabinet table, then the outcome is much greater than any one individual can achieve. Indeed, many have remarked upon how perhaps one of Bob Hawke's greatest contributions to Australian political life was the fact that he was the chairman of a cabinet, a cabinet in which there were many other talented individuals who contributed to the outcome of this country. Indeed, that cabinet was, by modern standards, a relatively small cabinet, but it was a talented cabinet and did great things over that period of time.

Successful reform comes from longevity in office, but longevity requires the trust of the Australian people. That trust of the Australian people only comes to leaders who are prepared to listen unceasingly to the Australian people, to know that ultimately we in this place must serve them, that we must meet the challenges of their everyday lives, not just now but the challenges that they'll meet in the future, and have a preparedness to argue for a proposed set of solutions that will address those challenges now and in the future. As other speakers have remarked, it was one of the outstanding achievements of Bob Hawke to unceasingly argue for the proposals that he thought were important to bring about the wellbeing, the welfare and the better economic and social outcomes for the people of Australia. We all stand in his stead in that regard.

As I said, I came here in the last months of the Hawke prime ministership. Paul Keating launched his first challenge to Bob Hawke, as I recall, at the end of June in 1991—I think I'd been here for a matter of two or three weeks when that happened. Over the course of that period of time when Paul Keating left the frontbench and sat up here on the backbench on this side until the second, successful, challenge occurred in December of that year was a difficult time for Bob Hawke as Prime Minister. It's, sadly, an iron law of Australian politics that two challenges to a Prime Minister will ultimately succeed—one can think of other occasions over succeeding years when that's been the case here in Australia. During that time, Bob Hawke nonetheless continued to argue his case.

I remember sitting on the other side of the chamber next to Wilson Tuckey. In one famous encounter between the Speaker and Tuckey—which occurred on a fairly regular occasion at that time—there were no time limits on answers to questions, and I think Bob had been going on for about 14 minutes in the answer to one question. Tuckey famously—some might say infamously—stood up and said to Leo McLeay, who was the Speaker, 'Mr Speaker, would you put him out of his misery?' It was probably unkind in the circumstances, but nonetheless it was a reflection of the willingness of debate which occurred. I say that because in discussions like this sometimes we can be sentimental about the past. Sometimes we can forget that what happens in here is nonetheless a willing debate about a series of issues. But what I think is important about Bob Hawke's legacy is that he saw the common good of this country and what the best outcome was, as far as he saw it, and he was prepared to argue it for this country.

Mr Deputy Speaker, each year I travel to your state to watch a certain bike race. When driving over there and back, it's become our family practice to stop at the Bordertown Hotel to have lunch on the way back to Melbourne. Every time I pass through Bordertown, I've thought about Bob Hawke and how this small country town, close to the border of South Australia and Victoria, produced one of our greatest leaders—obviously, Labor's greatest Prime Minister—and one of the greatest prime ministers of this country. I was pleased to read therefore that there's been put in place a proposal to purchase the house in which Bob Hawke was born and to turn it into a museum for Bob Hawke—and more broadly for the Labor Party—and the contribution that he made to this country.

I think that this is fitting. Far too often we fail in this country to recognise the contributions that leaders on all sides of politics have made to the wellbeing of this country. In Bob Hawke, we had a great leader. I'm proud to stand here, as someone who served for a very short period of time in this chamber when he was here, to recognise from the other side of politics that he made a great contribution to this country. I extend my condolences to his family, friends and the wider Labor movement in particular. May he rest in peace.

When JFK was killed in 1963, Bob Hawke was a young 33-year-old having his first crack at winning a seat here in the federal parliament. He was running then for the seat of Corio—a seat that's now held by my friend Richard Marles. And so the story goes that, apparently, a breathless campaign manager rushed into Bob Hawke's office and told him, 'Kennedy's dead!' To which, apparently, Bob Hawke went ashen white and said: 'What? Graham Kennedy is dead?' That's typical Bob. It tells you everything you need to know about the man. It's all of his innate Australianness captured in one sentence.

He loved a drink, and he was good at it; he even held a world record at one point. In 1955, when he was at Oxford University, he drank two and a half pints of beer in 11 seconds. About 50 years later, when he was in his 70s, he was back at Oxford University, then with another Rhodes Scholar—the former President of the United States, Bill Clinton—and he was asked to give it another go. Apparently Bill and Hillary were both yelling out, 'Go Bob!' He got it down then in 20 seconds. He was still doing the same sort of thing at the cricket until a few years ago.

Bob Hawke was no ordinary bloke. He was as at home at the hill at the SCG as he was dining with presidents. He was a larrikin statesman. A man with a big brain and an even bigger heart. He wasn't perfect—far from it—but he was honest about his flaws, and people loved him for it. Without question, he was one of the greatest Prime Ministers that this country has ever had.

Like so many others here in the parliament, I grew up in what we now call the 'Hawke era'. I grew up watching Bob on TV. He was the man with the funny voice and the big, wavy hair. The man so often with tears in his eyes and the man with the lairy jacket the morning that we won the America's Cup. But as I grew up, I saw a different Bob Hawke. I realised that he was also the man who gave my mum the green card that she got out whenever we got sick and went to the doctors. He was the man who meant a lot more kids, like me, from working-class suburbs finished high school. In 1983 only three in 10 kids finished high school in Australia. By the time that Bob Hawke was done more than 70 per cent did. I was part of that 70 per cent.

Bob's mum, Edith, used to tell him that education was the golden key that unlocked every door and Bob knew that. For a kid like me in the 1980s, the first in my family to finish Year 10—let alone Year 12—I knew that what he did was life changing. Bob was also the man that brought my wife's grandparents from Vietnam to Australia and allowed them to settle here and start a new life in the best country in the world—part of the family reunion program.

He didn't just change the lives of individuals; he changed the whole country. We are a very different place today from the country that we were 40 years ago. In 1980, Lee Kuan Yew, former Prime Minister of Singapore, said that Australia was destined to become the 'poor white trash' of Asia. That didn't happen. It didn't happen because of a lot of things that Bob Hawke and Paul Keating did. Things like floating the dollar, cutting tariffs, opening up our economy and enmeshing ourselves with Asia. None of that was easy. It is easy to sit back here today and think that all of that was, but the truth is it only happened because people like Bob, Paul and great union leaders like Bill Kelty fought for it, argued the case for it and persuaded others that we needed it. Not all of it was supported by the coalition. Not all of it was supported by our own side. Bob, Paul and Bill were called sellouts by some.

It's not easy to hold your nerve and keep driving forward when people are hurting and industries are buckling under the strain of all of it. We are lucky that they did, because what they did remade Australia—one with more jobs, more businesses and more opportunities than ever existed when I was a boy.

Bob also abhorred racism. He mercilessly shamed those who attempted to use it for political advantage here in this place and right across Australia. He helped tear down what Robert Kennedy called the 'walls of oppression and resistance' overseas. The economic sanctions that he convinced other countries to sign up to helped to bring down apartheid in South Africa. When Nelson Mandela visited parliament almost 30 years ago he told Bob that he was here at this time because of him.

For a party that I love so much, Bob also taught us how to win and how to govern—something we often need reminding of. As Paul Kelly said in his book The Hawke Ascendancy:

The great strength of the Hawke government was the success in capturing that balance between reform and reassurance. Herein lay Hawke's genius. He encamped squarely in the middle of Australian politics and constructed a coalition spanning business on the right and unions on the left. The old Labor Party aligned itself with labour against capital. The Liberal Party was in alliance with capital against Labor. But Hawke sought a compromise which would make the ALP under his leadership the natural majority party.

In the last few years, like many other colleagues on this side of the House, I got to spend a bit of time with the man I grew up watching on TV. As others have said, meeting your heroes can be a scary thing, but not Bob. He was always very kind to me. He was always very generous with his time, inviting me back time and again for a cup of coffee. He was always very generous with his advice—always keen to talk about politics, always keen to talk about the party, always keen to talk about the future. He was also very funny. He liked to tell jokes, none of which I can repeat here in the parliament! He always asked about my wife, Louise, and he would often remark that the age gap between me and Louise was the same as between him and Blanche. We also used to talk a bit about death. It struck me that whenever we did, he always seemed very much at peace with it, whenever it was to come. I remember saying to him, 'Maybe, Bob, that's because you know that even after you're gone much of you will remain. All the things that you did will last many years after you're gone.' I think he knew that. I could tell by the look in his eye that he knew that.

There are lots of us who yearn for another Bob Hawke. I can understand that. But people like Bob are like Halley's comet—they don't come around that often. I miss him terribly, but I know that instead of mourning him and mourning what we've lost, or hoping somewhat vainly for some kind of political reincarnation some time soon, instead we should take a look around and just be grateful for what he's left us. He's the green card in our pockets. He's the superannuation we will retire with. He's the architect of so much of what is modern day Australia. His invisible fingerprints are all around us, and we're a better country for it. As Blanche said in her book about Bob a few years ago, 'Even though his service to our country is at an end, the long tail of its comet still shines.'

It is with great pleasure that I rise to speak on the condolence motion for the Hon. Robert James Lee Hawke. First of all, I want to associate myself with the comments this morning from both the Prime Minister and the member for Grayndler. They were two outstanding tributes to an outstanding Australian. I also congratulate my friend the member for Blaxland for his contribution. Unlike the member for Blaxland, when I grew up Bob Hawke wasn't really regarded very fondly in my house of Country Party voters. They didn't regard him with quite the same reverence that maybe they did in your household but he was respected across the board. Even in the staunch Country Party home of my parents in Sale. They grudgingly acknowledged that Bob got things done.

Today, as we rise and make comments, it's important that we reflect on a life very well lived, a life of great achievements. Certainly, when it comes to Robert James Lee Hawke, or Bob Hawke, or Hawkie as everyone knew him, it is an extraordinary life that we are reflecting on. He was a giant of politics in his era. I grew up attending high school during that era, and he was a giant of his era in terms of dominating the political stage. Australians loved him for his authenticity and his larrikin spirit. They could all see a bit of themselves in Bob. It's tempting to think of him only in that way, until you start reflecting on the reforming government he led and the achievements, of which there were many.

I'm going to talk a little about his role in the veterans area, given my responsibility for veterans' affairs. Politics is often just a lot about numbers, and for Bob Hawke the numbers were impressive: only 12 years in office, but he was Prime Minister for almost nine of those years, making him the third longest-serving Prime Minister in Australia's history. He was 50 when he entered parliament, which is old by today's standards. A lot of members start much earlier than that, but he was 50, and it was his second attempt, as we've just heard. He entered parliament in 1980, but by early 1983 he was already the Leader of the Opposition for just one month, after which he became Prime Minister—probably the most remarkably short parliamentary apprenticeship of anyone, because he reached such a high level in such a short amount of time.

But the numbers don't really do justice to Bob Hawke. His achievements across the board—of which we've heard many today—extended to the area of veterans' affairs, and the legacy is something we should regard positively now. Many of the achievements of Prime Minister Hawke continue to benefit veterans today, in 2019. The introduction of Medicare in 1984 offered repatriation beneficiaries the choice of having their health care provided through the repatriation treatment system or Medicare, a very direct benefit to our veteran community. In 1986, the Hawke government implemented the Veterans' Entitlements Act, and that was a key piece of veterans legislation which consolidated all the repatriation legislation and removed some of the anomalies, such as cancellation of the war widows pension on remarriage, to allow those widows who remarried to retain their pension. In 1988, with the introduction of the Safety, Rehabilitation and Compensation Act, the compensation policy for veterans of peacetime service shifted towards rehabilitation and return to the workforce, which today we recognise as being critical elements of the overall health and wellbeing of our veteran community. And, perhaps most notably, in 1988 the gold card was extended to female veterans with qualifying service from World War II, and, in recognition of some of the past discrimination in relation to issues such as pay and access to defence service homes assistance, no age qualification was needed. I guess that, for our Vietnam veterans, the Hawke era will be remembered very much for the 'welcome home' parade for our Vietnam veterans, which occurred in Sydney in 1987. A crowd of 100,000 Australians, including the Prime Minister, Bob Hawke, was there to help try and heal the divisions which existed between our Vietnam veterans and the Australian community at that time. So, in many ways, the Hawke legacy to the veteran community was something rather substantial.

In defence, Prime Minister Hawke in that time sought to strengthen the ANZUS alliance within a greater framework of Australian defence self-reliance, and he was the Prime Minister who commenced the development of the domestically built Collins class submarines and established AUSMIN, which is a routine high-level political engagement on defence and security matters with the United States. So, whether it's in defence or in veterans, Prime Minister Hawke's legacy is something quite substantial.

But, for me personally, I think the Hawke legacy around Landcare is something for which this generation and future generations should remain eternally grateful. It was Bob Hawke who in July 1989 made the initial commitment of $340 million over 10 years to establish Landcare. At that time, Bob Hawke said:

When the earth is spoiled, humanity and all living things are diminished. We have taken too much from the earth and given back too little. It's time to say enough is enough.

He said:

… we are declaring next year the Year of Landcare …

As I said, I think this generation and future generations owe him an eternal debt of gratitude for that decision, with this year seeing the 30th anniversary of Landcare. It actually arose from an unlikely collaboration where a joint submission from the National Farmers' Federation and the Australian Conservation Foundation led to this very practical approach to managing environmental issues. I'm very pleased that this government has committed an additional $100 million in the 2019-20 budget to a new Environment Restoration Fund, which will build on our $1 billion National Landcare Program.

By any measure, Bob Hawke's parliamentary career was a life of incredible service, and I guess it challenges those of us who serve here today to consider: what lessons can those of us honoured to be in the 46th Parliament, in this very first week of the 46th Parliament, learn from Bob Hawke's parliamentary career?

I'd like to leave that thought to the man himself to answer, because, when he was asked how he'd like to be remembered, Bob Hawke said:

As a bloke who loved his country, still does. And loves Australians and who wasn't essentially changed by high office.

I think that's the critical point: 'wasn't essentially changed by high office'. I'd have to say to Bob Hawke: mission accomplished—and incredibly important advice to all those who serve in this place today. On behalf of the people of Gippsland, I'd like to extend my condolences to Bob Hawke's family, to his friends, and to his Labor Party brothers and sisters. May he rest in peace.

It is fitting that we're having a day of tribute, in speeches, to Bob Hawke. I think he would be pretty chuffed by that as well, and I'd like to think that perhaps he's listening in from on high to all these words about him today. It was obviously a very bittersweet moment when we heard the news of Bob's dying. It is a time of great grief for the loss of the great man, a figure who was larger than life and who seemed ever-present throughout our lives, but it also is a time of great reflection and celebration of someone who has been a pretty extraordinary influence not just on the politics of Australia but on pretty much every aspect; our modern economy and our social services system have been built on the back of his legacy.

Of course, as you've heard from many of the members here in this place, Bob and his voice, his actions, his words, are the soundtrack to the lives of many of us, and the soundtrack, for many of us, to our political awakening as well. I remember being at high school at the time of Bob's prime ministership and I remember one of the lovely radical Catholic nuns, who was teaching us English, talking about the accord—to a group of young women in a Catholic girls school—and saying, 'This is amazing. This is an extraordinary time. Labor—Labor, of all parties—is sitting down with industry and is negotiating an accord about how to grow the economy and have a social safety net for our workforce.' And she was saying, 'Karl Marx would be spinning in his grave! This is such a historic moment.' I remember that. I remember my year 12 HSC Legal Studies exam question was on the Franklin Dam case. This is the sort of backdrop to the political awakening of many in this current crop of politicians. So the emotion and passion that you hear in this place about Bob is also about our own growing-up and our own political awakening.

We as a country are so fortunate that we had Robert James Lee Hawke as our Prime Minister and that he took the decision at such an early age to engage actively in a political life. That, of course, came at some cost to his kids, and to his beautiful wife, Hazel, at the time, as well. We owe a great debt to the Hawke family for allowing their dad, not often at their choice—and growing up in very difficult circumstances—to continue to have such a public political life.

Hawke changed our country for the better. He set us on a new path. He gave us a new confidence and a new place in the world. His government opened our economy to the world. He floated the dollar. He pushed so many environmental agendas, saving the Franklin. He reset industrial relations. He outlawed gender discrimination in the workforce. He welcomed Chinese students after Tiananmen, and spoke up for human rights and humanity. He abhorred racism at home and overseas, and he worked tirelessly to confront poverty and address Indigenous disadvantage.

When Bob entered government, 30 per cent of Australians finished year 12. That seems extraordinary today—only 30 per cent of Australian children finished year 12. When he left office, 70 per cent did. That's a huge legacy, children finishing their secondary education and going on to do great things in and of their own right. Of course, in my previous portfolio, one where the legacy is writ large every single day when people visit their GP, is Medicare—that green card we all carry in our wallets, in our purses or in our pockets; a very important universal health insurance scheme for all Australians. And he said very much at the time when Medicare was being introduced that, without Medicare, there were some two million Australians who, in the event of a significant health event, would go bankrupt because of their health costs, and that was the reason Medicare needed to be introduced—fought by the opposite side but now embedded well and truly into our nation's polity.

I could stand here and talk for a long time about the many, many achievements of Hawke in the way in which he transformed our nation, and I think a lot of people have contributed in that way, but, beyond his policy achievements, I think there is something really remarkable about the man both during and post politics—about the way in which he kept that passion and fire alive but also was able to embed that in others. The Hawke government was the first Labor government that I had the privilege of voting for, once I had reached the age of 18. And, when I first ran for office back in 2001, Bob came to Ballarat to help. He'd been many times to our city before that, but he was picked up in Melbourne and driven down the Western Highway to Ballarat to campaign with me. There are many incidents and anecdotes that come from that, but, in particular, I really noticed that, wherever we walked, people wanted to come and talk to Bob. But not only that; they actually wanted to grab his hand, to touch him, to hug him and surround him, and they wanted to say things like, 'I was able to go to university,' 'I was able to stay well,' or, 'I had cancer and this is what happened to me.' They wanted to tell him about what the things his government had done had meant for them deeply and personally in their own lives. And that is really Bob's legacy.

He was also an incredibly funny man—just raucous, larger than life. Some of the things he did! I remember walking through our Wendouree Village—it's now called Stockland Shopping Centre—with him, walking along and suddenly noticing he was no longer walking with me. He'd darted off. I looked over, and there he was in the bookshop that was there at the time, flicking through, of course, the Guinness book of records, and at the top of his voice saying, 'Yep, still there. My beer-drinking record is still there,' much to the humour of the crowd that had gathered around him. He wanted to talk about how that was still there, and that made us laugh.

In particular, there was his incredible generosity of mentoring others. He saw very much his role as an elder statesman within the Australian Labor Party. He took that very seriously, campaigning with the likes of me in my first election campaign but taking the time to stop and to talk to young people in the labour movement, spending time continuously with the ACTU and with new parliamentarians to teach us, to engender that passion for politics with all of us. Before Bob headed back down the highway in that first '01 election campaign, my campaign office was in a 100-year-old bakery—at the time, there was a big oven still upstairs. I remember him heading upstairs; he wanted to have a cigar before he headed off down the highway. He sat and held court, as Bob could do, with all of the young Labor people who had come and were helping out in my election campaign, and for an hour and a half to two hours they were transfixed, asking him: 'What are the important values? What are the things, the policies, we should be pursuing?' And he just took his time with every one of them to talk that through. I know many of them who now have gone on to be very senior staffers within the labour movement—politicians themselves. They talk about that hour or two that they had with Bob sitting around the ovens of the old bakery as being a really important part of their own political awakening.

Anyone who's met Bob would have a story just like that: that generosity, that humanity that he would pass on to everybody. So, from the people of Ballarat in particular, I do want to say we are so sad about Bob's passing. We're custodians of the Prime Minister's avenue of honour in the Ballarat Botanical Gardens, and there was an impromptu gathering. Unfortunately, I was unable to be there, but on the night that Bob passed away there was an impromptu gathering in the Ballarat Botanical Gardens in front of his statue. The group came together there to share Bob's life, to share a beer and to consider what Bob meant to them. All of those present knew that their country and their lives had been changed by the actions, the decisions and the changes brought in by the government that Bob led.

One of the things that Bob was very much known for was running a very consultative cabinet process, allowing his ministers—and he had many talented ministers—to actually do the work and the policy work that was required. He really gave them their head, allowing them to develop that work. It is why people who worked with him in that era—Neal Blewett, Kim Beazley—speak of him with great fondness and strength. They know that they did their best work because they had the best leader at the time.

Bob said that he wanted to be remembered as a bloke who loved his country and still does, who loves Australians and who wasn't essentially changed by high office, and I think we do remember him in exactly that way. The Bob I met campaigning back in 2001 was the same Bob I remember from my childhood. It was that love that drove him to make our nation a better place for all of us. It is why Australians, in turn, loved him. It's why people came up to him in the street and wanted to talk to him and tell him about what he had done for them.

Bob wanted to be remembered for that but, in reality, he will, of course, be remembered for more. As Treasurer, Paul Keating once said:

The fact of the matter is that the Labor Party makes the political heroes in this country.

Reading that quote almost 30 years later, it is impossible not to think of Bob and, of course, of Paul. Bob epitomised the need to drive our country forward, that the world would not wait for us and that government is the best tool—imperfect as it can be—with which to give Australians better lives. That is the purpose that drives us all. All of us in this place should continually strive to live up to those standards, to be the representatives that Australians deserve and to have the courage of conviction to do what needs to be done.

To Blanche, to Bob's children, to the late Hazel Hawke and to all of his family and his friends, I pass on my sincere condolences. Our country was very lucky to have Bob, and his legacy will last long. Particularly to Blanche I want to say: it is an extraordinary gift to be with someone in their weakest moments, but to be able to be with that person and allow them to live the fullest of lives every single day as their body fails is an extraordinary thing, an extraordinary act of compassion and love. I want to pay tribute to her particularly, and to Bob's children, not only for allowing him to die with such extraordinary dignity, surrounded by love, but also for continuing with the huge legacy that he has given to this fantastic country of ours. Vale, Bob.

I want to take this opportunity to join with my colleagues in paying tribute to Australia's 23rd Prime Minister, Bob Hawke. We've heard an array of amazing stories and anecdotes of the former Prime Minister today, and it is a fitting occasion for our parliament to set aside all other business to recognise the outstanding contribution that he made to our country and the enduring legacy that, I'm sure, he and his family are proud of and will continue to be proud of.

What an amazing life! He was born in Bordertown, went on to become a Rhodes scholar, was president of the ACTU, had a very short-lived period as Leader of the Opposition and then, ultimately, was Prime Minister of Australia. He had many academic achievements in his time at Oxford, some of which were augmented by his drinking abilities. He very famously drank 2½ pints of beer in 11 seconds, which I understand made it into the Guinness book of records. I suspect that probably had some contribution to his early political success and popularity.

He passed up several opportunities to enter parliament before finally becoming the member for Wills. Having had many family members who lived in his electorate at that time from a Lebanese background, I can say the way he represented that community and many other migrant communities is something that to this day endures within those communities and the fact that he had an Italian pensioners' club named after him. What a great reflection of just how important he was to so many of those new Australians, and that has never been forgotten.

In many respects I think Bob Hawke reflects the best of what many call 'old Labor', and he really did seek in everything he did to represent the best interests of working Australians. He knew what made the average Aussie tick. I know that's a generalisation, but I think he really did understand what Australian people found important, what they wanted to see in a leader and he was easily able to relate to people. He wasn't aloof, he wasn't out of touch, and I think what he exuded so powerfully throughout his life was the fact he never saw himself above the average Australian punter, to use a well-worn phrase.

His famous 1983 election slogan was: 'Bob Hawke. Bringing Australia Together.' And, boy, in many respects, that's exactly what he did as Prime Minister. In fact, 'Advance Australia Fair', which was first adopted as our national anthem in 1984 by a proclamation of the Governor-General at the time, Sir Ninian Stephen, was on the recommendation of his Labor government. In many respects that was a reflection of the ethos: Bringing Australia Together.

Many have spoken about the fact that Bob Hawke really was the catalyst, along with his Treasurer, for opening up the Australian economy, especially to Asia; floating the dollar; reforming the tax system; and privatising several large government owned businesses—many of which had the support of the Liberal opposition at the time. However, these were great achievements that I know, from many of the interviews I saw throughout his life, were a legacy that he was very proud of.

Again, it's been remarked on numerous occasions from this side of the House today—but I suspect many of us in the Liberal Party also believe—that Bob Hawke was Labor's best Prime Minister by a long shot, and I think his record proves that. I think his connection to the Australian electorate and his ability to win four elections also prove that.

Bob Hawke was also a major player on the international stage. He served our nation with distinction, forging close defence and security ties with a political hero of many of those on this side of the House, including me, Ronald Reagan, and of cordial foreign relations with another global giant of that era, Margaret Thatcher and the UK.

Australians will never forget the legacy and significant achievements that Bob Hawke gave to our nation. This building that we're in is a lasting testament to his foresight and vision for a modern Australia, amongst many other much more important things than the bricks and mortar of this building, but this is a visual representation of that.

Can I express my deepest condolences and sympathy to his wife, Blanche, his children and grandchildren. I also recognise the late Hazel Hawke, who, for many people, was absolutely a part of the Hawke duo of his time as Prime Minister. Hazel Hawke was almost as well loved, if not more so, than Bob during those years. I can certainly attest to that from my own family's experience.

As the Prime Minister recalled earlier today, Bob's mother repeatedly found herself drawn to the words of the prophet Isaiah when she was pregnant with him:

For unto us a Child is born,

Unto us a Son is given;

And the government will be upon His shoulder.

How prophetic that was to become in his life and to the betterment of our country. May he rest in peace.

Bob Hawke loved Australia and Australians loved Bob Hawke. Like no other Prime Minister, he personified, perfectly, the Australian culture and spirit—the larrikin leader with a big heart and a unique ability to endear people to his values, his beliefs and his party's policies. He rose to the highest office in our nation as Robert James Lee Hawke, but he was never, ever above the people. Bob was always with Australians, and Hawkie will always be with us. He was a man in tune with the Australian psyche, with a plan for a better country, a determination to deliver it and a charisma to win support for it.

When I think about the Hawke era, the overwhelming emotion I feel is pride, pride in Australia and its people. Bob Hawke made us proud to be Australian. Bob and his government established a confidence in Australia and its future that we haven't seen since that era of the 1980s. He campaigned in 1983 on a platform of Bringing Australia Together, but his legacy shows that Bob Hawke helped put modern Australia together. Throughout the 1980s he was Australia. It's almost incredible that wherever you go, across this wide country, almost every Australian has a Bob Hawke story, either of meeting him or of being at a particular place, remembering where you were on a particular day—particularly when we won the America's Cup in 1983 and Bob uttered those immortal words that are now part of Australian folklore history and culture.

In his early life, Bob developed a value system based on equality, advancement through education, opposition to racism, and social justice through collective rights in the workplace. When it came to these values, he never, ever waivered from them in his career in the trade union movement, and in parliament and as our nation's leader. He was proud of his trade union background. Bob loved the history of the Australian union movement and all that it stood for. This came through whenever you went to a union function with Bob Hawke. Inevitably, he was up the front leading the singing of 'Solidarity Forever'. He knew every one of the words and led that song with vigour and purpose.

In his career in the union movement he used his intellect and supreme oratory skills to argue, on the national stage, for better wages and conditions for working Australians. Many of those wages and conditions that we take for granted today—things like long service leave, sick leave, annual leave and overtime rates—were established by Bob Hawke. He argued before the Industrial Relations Commission for those conditions to be put into Australian awards and workplace agreements because it was the right thing to do by Australian workers, and our economy could afford it.

Bob was the master negotiator, able to lead a discussion to consensus and advancement for the workers that he was representing. No wonder he was the figurehead and leader of the Australian Council of Trade Unions for over a decade. He understood, better than any other, the value of the Australian union movement to advancing the economic and social interests of Australians. He led a government that modernised the Australian economy and laid the foundations for the economic growth and living conditions that we all enjoy in Australia today. He led a government that saw Australia become an open-trading nation, that reduced barriers to trade, that introduced competition into industry, that modernised industry. He introduced the Prices and Incomes Accord, which introduced the notion of the social wage, with workers forgoing wage increases for social advancement through the establishment of Medicare and superannuation, the compulsory retirement income system. He introduced enterprise bargaining, the notion of productivity based bargaining in workplaces, which was unheard of in Australia at the time, which had a very rigid and industry based award system for conditions and the establishment of wages through awards.

He was deeply interested in and saw the value of education. There's an often quoted statistic, one that I think perfectly highlights Bob's commitment and passion for education and the advances that his government made for education in this country. When he took office as Prime Minister in 1983, Australia had one of the lowest school retention rates in the developed world—that is, the rate of people going on from school in year 10 to complete high school at year 12. At that time, 30 per cent of all students were going on to complete high school in year 12. By the time his government had finished, that figure had risen to 70 per cent. What a difference that made to productivity advancement in Australia. It was his government that kickstarted the productivity surge in Australia that has lasted for decades. As I said earlier, his government laid the foundation for the economic growth that has seen Australia record the world's longest continuous period of growth, which is still running today. The Australian economy of today is a Labor economy. It has been the world's best performing economy of the last quarter of a century because it was built on the notion of fairness and the value of a progressive taxation system, decent workplace conditions and wages, a living wage, and a system of government payments that were means tested and supported uplifting Australians and supported education.

Bob fought for equality his whole life. He abhorred racism and discrimination, taking the fight to apartheid and bringing many more from across the world with him. He was a champion for women's equality, developing a national agenda for women and establishing the Affirmative Action Agency. The Sex Discrimination Act 1984 put outside the law gender discrimination in workplaces. His list of achievements to protect and preserve Australia's environment and, importantly, on the international stage include preventing mining in Antarctica; the saving of the Franklin River, which still flows in its natural form today; recognising the threat of climate change; and establishing and growing national parks throughout the country is one of legend.

Of course, the Hawke cabinet that made a lot of these decisions was one of the best. Bob's style of cabinet was to allow his cabinet ministers to do their jobs—to do the research, to do the consultation and to come to cabinet with submissions—and if the submissions were well researched, well consulted and in the interest of Australia he would back his cabinet ministers to the hilt. Something that people in the electorate that I represent, Kingsford Smith's, are very proud of is the fact that one of my predecessors as member for Kingsford Smith, Lionel Bowen, was Bob Hawke's Deputy Prime Minister for many years. In a tribute dinner last decade that Bob spoke at in honour of Lionel Bowen, he described Lionel as 'pure crystal', someone who was always to be relied upon and was always there as a loyal deputy and a constant source of good advice. They had a friendship that lasted for many decades and was very much valued by Bob Hawke.

Like so many who have spoken before me, I have a Bob Hawke story. Bob came to the launch of my first campaign for Kingsford Smith, in 2013, to officially launch my campaign. He was 84 years old at the time, but he'd not lost it one bit. He fired up and got the crowd of hundreds riled up about the potential of a coalition government and what it would do to this country. I might say that much of what Bob Hawke spoke of that day about cuts to services and the like has come true.

But the incredible thing about Bob at that campaign launch—there were hundreds of people there—was that when it concluded everyone wanted to come up and touch him and shake his hand and tell their Bob Hawke story and how it made a difference to their lives. It literally took him over an hour just to get out of the venue, because everyone wanted a photo with him, everyone wanted to talk to him, everyone wanted a piece of Bob Hawke, and he was willing to oblige. Although he was tired, it was late in the day, and he had been doing some campaigning earlier in the morning—we're talking about a man in his 80s—it seemed to boost him; it seemed to lift him. He got energy out of being with other Australians and hearing their stories about his era of government. It's something I will never forget. I'm very appreciative of Bob Hawke for coming to my campaign launch in 2013, and I believe it's one of the reasons why I did well in that particular campaign.

Something else that Kingsford Smith is proud to boast of is that the Hawke legend continues in my electorate to this day, because Hawke's Brewing Company—we all know that now there's a beer named after Bob Hawke—was founded in Coogee, in the electorate of Kingsford Smith, by two young fellas, Nathan and Luke. They took the inspiration from their hero, Bob Hawke, to start an Australian beer company built on the values of that national icon. I ran into Nathan and Luke, believe it or not, on election day 2019 down at Coogee, where they were going to vote. I was on the booth handing out how-to-votes. They said to me that after voting they were going over to visit Blanche to pay their respects to her and the family not only for the legacy that Bob had left, but also for him agreeing to be the patron, if you like—the name—for their brewing company, which is doing so well. In the wake of that I said to my staff, 'For our election party tonight, I want you to try to buy a few cases of Hawke's lager so we can all do a toast to Hawkie tonight.' They spent an hour or so scouring every single bottle shop throughout Sydney. Guess what? There wasn't one can left. Bob Hawke's lager had sold out across Sydney. I think that says everything about how much Australians loved Bob Hawke.

Bob Hawke loved a beer, of course, but he loved Australia and its people even more and that made him a committed republican. For him, cutting the symbolic ties with the United Kingdom and having an Australian as our head of state was inevitable. During the 1999 referendum campaign for an Australian head of state, he argued that we didn't need more time to mature as a nation. Australia was and is a mature nation. We were ready then and we're still ready now. While an Australian republic may now seem far from inevitable, we remember the long-term vision of Bob Hawke and his unshakeable faith in Australia and Australians. That is what came through in that 1999 referendum campaign—his faith in Australia and Australians.

He leaves a huge legacy. He leaves so much for us all as parliamentarians to aspire to not only for what we can achieve as individuals but, more importantly, what we can achieve together as a parliament. I offer my sincerest condolences to Blanche and his family. Rest in peace, Hawkie.

I rise to add my condolences to those who have spoken so well about the life of former Prime Minister Bob Hawke. I was privileged enough to be invited by Blanche d'Alpuget to the funeral of Bob—obviously Bob didn't invite me. There's something that can be said about the way the Labor Party sends off one of their champions; it was an incredible event. I just want to put on the record the endorsement that was given by so many. Probably the most memorable speech was that of Kim Beazley, which really encapsulated the strengths of his friend and colleague. It was also good to see, at that service, the capacity for old adversaries to be brought together. The reception that was given to former Prime Minister Paul Keating also made sure that an adornment of that service was the bringing together of the ends to once more make a circle, so that there would be a sense of parting, in the relationship between Keating and Hawke, in the way it commenced, which was one of friendship.

As a man from Bordertown in South Australia, Bob Hawke had the capacity to encapsulate both the urbane and the regional and this carried him well through his career. He was also a Rhodes scholar, as were so many other former leaders: Kim Beazley, Tony Abbott, Malcolm Turnbull. He was also someone in whom—when people watched him on television and saw him from a distance, as people in the country did—people had a sense of pride, because he was a person who obviously had a strong intellect, and this was conveyed through a larrikin persona, but nonetheless that intellect gave people a sense of pride in his capacity to connect, especially to world leaders such as President Bush. When people observed from a distance, they could see that there was a real affinity, a real connection—that Australia, through the voice of Bob Hawke, had a capacity to reach out to other major players in the world and make us as one, and not to make us look like an appendage. He was a person who walked on level ground with these global leaders.

Also, he no doubt made Australia grow up.
