For so long, we had had a sentimentality towards England, an attachment to England, which, obviously, in the time that Bob Hawke came about, was becoming a bit of an anachronism, and we had to make some changes. The most notable one was the Australia Act, which of course cut ties to the Privy Council. When you think about it, in 1983 it was pretty ridiculous: the idea that you were going to go and sit on a wool sack in London somewhere and make an appeal about an issue in Australia was rather absurd. And, even though there was a sense that things should remain as they were, Bob Hawke had the capacity to shake the nation and say: 'It's time to move on a bit. We can stand on our own two feet now.'

Would Bob Hawke survive today? Probably not. What we've also seen is the advent of mobile phones and the 24/7 media, where this has turned more into cheap daytime television than politics, in some instances. As to a person with the credentials of Bob Hawke but also the colour of Bob Hawke, it would be a hard ask to say whether he would survive. I noted one of the articles by Phil Coorey where he sort of disputed that, and I would say that it is a shame that the world has passed where the John F Kennedys and the Eisenhowers and the Bob Hawkes would actually be able to survive in a 24/7 media spotlight, and that what that would've meant for places like Australia is that we wouldn't have had a person like Bob Hawke, and we wouldn't have had what he did for Australia, which was to create an epiphany—a greater realisation that we can stand on our own two feet and be proud of who we are.

In closing, I'd just like to give my own experiences of Bob Hawke. I found him to give sage advice. I had the pleasure of meeting Bob Hawke a number of times. And the one thing that is not just specific to Bob Hawke but to a number of politicians is: as people ascend their political mountain, their political Mount Sinai, they go up as purists; they go up as absolutists; they go up with this sort of fervent belief. But, once they can see the vision and the view from the top, they descend more as realists; they descend more as people who can see and give credit to the strengths of both sides of the political fence. They're probably more honest in their political views and can sit you down—as I did once with Bob, I remember, at a lounge in Beijing—and give you an honest appraisal of politics and give you advice as if they were on your side of the political fence. So they were doing it with the best intentions at heart and on your behalf, to try and see politics through the eyes of people on your side and to try to point out what they think were pertinent issues to you. That is about it.

I'd like to convey, as everybody has, to the family of Bob my deepest sympathies. Even though she's passed, I'd like to also once more commend the great work that Hazel did; she was greatly loved by the Australian people. I'd also like to commend the great care and love that was given by Blanche d'Alpuget to Bob. It was something that I noted personally in discussions with Vikki over the last part of Bob's life, that she really was a person who stepped up to the mark and made sure that the final part of his life was one of dignity, one of continued involvement with his great love of politics and other parts of life and, obviously, crosswords and other things he did on his front porch. I'd also like to commend her and thank her for the work that she did.

As I think Kim Beazley said when he was closing, we're not quite sure where Bob finished. He was an agnostic, but where he resided at the end no-one will ever know—I suppose it's the same for all of us. But Kim Beazley said in his eulogy he's absolutely certain that a person with the character of Bob Hawke would be taken into the arms of our Lord and looked after.

I am very sad but also pleased that I am getting this opportunity to speak on this condolence motion. I'm sure, like most Australians, everyone has their own reasons to love Bob Hawke. There is no doubt that Bob Hawke looms large in Australia as an Australian Prime Minister that was very much loved but also deeply respected. There are many Australians, including myself, who are so grateful for Bob Hawke and his reformist agenda. Many people have benefited from everything he introduced. Whether it's the introduction of Medicare, a gift of affordable universal healthcare that still every Australian benefits from; the introduction of superannuation and the dignity in retirement; the support for workers, their families; the support for education; for improving the environment; for an outward-looking foreign policy, it's clear that Bob Hawke as Prime Minister achieved what we all want to achieve here—that is, leave Australia in a better position for future generations.

Bob really understood that a healthy economy must work for both employees and employers and that a strong safety net is critical to a healthy nation. He gave workers what they deserved, a voice, and demonstrated that with cooperation with government, workers and business, we can achieve not only a fairer society but also a more productive society. His economic reforms with Paul Keating at the time, while difficult, did not get universal support but they have set our nation on a course of uninterrupted economic growth. But Bob was not only respected for his principles and what he delivered but was loved for his nature, his down-to-earth charismatic nature which drew people in.

Bob had the ability to connect with everyday Australians. This connection did not finish when he ceased being Prime Minister but continued in the decades after. He continues to be revered by many who were too young to personally remember his time as Prime Minister but instinctively understand what a national treasure he was. His ability to connect with anyone was demonstrated in so many stories that we've heard in this place today and in the media in the days and weeks after his passing. This story has been told a few times already, but it was one that I witnessed firsthand, so I would like to recount it. It was at the end of a football match at the MCG as part of the American-Australian dialogue, when I was walking back with a group, including Bob and some of the American delegates, to catch a bus back to our hotel. The bus had not arrived, so we were standing there making small talk when we heard some yelling from a car stopped at the lights about 50 metres away. There were some young occupants, not much older than 20 years of age, who recognised Bob Hawke and began cheering and probably yelling out some other lewd comments. To the American delegation's amazement, rather than ignoring this carload of young men, Bob, not wanting to wait for the bus any longer, made a beeline through the traffic to the car, with his personal assistant running behind him, and jumped into the car before the lights turned green. The American ambassador at the time turned to me and asked, 'Isn't that your former president that got in the car?' I replied, 'Yes, it was,' to his amazement. He followed up, 'Well, that just would not happen in the United States.' This event not only highlighted the cultural differences between our nations but highlighted what was so special about Bob Hawke: his down-to-earth nature, his ability to connect with anyone, his popularity that had been passed down to the next generation, and probably his ability to get what he wanted as well. Needless to say, Bob did make it back to the hotel before the bus arrived to pick us up, so he certainly was able to deliver there.

Bob's love for the Labor Party was also something that never diminished when he ceased being Prime Minister. Indeed, as we've heard from so many stories in this place, Bob campaigned with so many of us in many elections. For me, he campaigned in 2007, 2010 and 2013. Of course, I knew that Bob was immensely popular, but seeing it in person was a sight to behold. On one of my early campaigns, I took Bob to Colonnades shopping centre, a local centre in my electorate in southern Adelaide. We were mobbed. Everyone loved him. Everyone wanted to talk to him, and he wanted to talk to them. In that situation, it would be almost understandable for Bob to become distracted by the attention, but, as yet another reflection of his nature, he remained completely focused on why he was there: campaigning for me. He insisted that no elector could get a photo of him unless I was also in the photo, remarking to me, 'Well, that's why I'm here.' So, needless to say, every elector who was in Colonnades shopping centre has a picture of Bob Hawke in their photo album, along with me standing in the background. But I did learn quickly in the 2007 campaign, Bob did not suffer fools. After sitting down with a young journalist from a local paper, Bob could not hide his exasperation when the journalist asked him, 'So what brings you to Seaford shops?' to which he replied. 'Well, I think it's pretty obvious: there's an election on.'

I will always remember Bob Hawke for his humour, his generosity, the time that he gave to me, his intelligence, his courage and, above all, his deep, unwavering love for Australians. My condolences to Blanche, his family, his extended family and his friends. He will be sorely missed.

Today we honour the loss of one of the political giants of our nation, Australia's third longest-serving Prime Minister, an incredible reformer, an intellect and a great, authentic Australian. I never met Bob Hawke, but, perhaps like most Australians, I feel as if I know the man quite intimately. He was in some respects the only Prime Minister that I knew growing up. He became the Prime Minister when I first started secondary school and remained so well into my university years. My first recollection of him was, of course, back in 1983 in that triumphant victory that Australia enjoyed, with Australia II winning the America's Cup against the Americans and Bob Hawke encapsulating the Australian spirit early in the morning, wearing his flamboyant jacket emblazoned with the words of Australia and really just capturing the essence of the moment for all of us.

He was the type of person that could do that, bringing Australia together for those key moments. Of course, when he first became Prime Minister, as has been mentioned by many people in this chamber, he campaigned on the motto and the theme of bringing Australians together. That indeed was one of the core characteristics that he brought to his prime ministership and to the character of who he was. It was one of the reasons that Australia was enamoured with him and loved him but also one of the reasons he was able to achieve so much as Prime Minister. The keywords that he was associated with were things like 'consensus', 'accord' and 'working together'. It was in this way, working with the Australian people, with unions, with businesses and others, that he did indeed achieve so much for our nation.

Bob Hawke is perhaps best known for, and I think his greatest legacy as Prime Minister of this country is, his economic reforms. They were indeed an impressive set of economic reforms. Think back about those reforms that he led: floating the dollar; deregulating the financial systems; admitting foreign banks to this country for the first time; dismantling many of the tariffs; starting the privatisation of government owned businesses; microeconomic reform in partnership with the states; and retirement incomes for all workers. Every single one of those reforms in itself is substantial. Together they completely transformed the Australian economy, opening up the Australian economy to the world and setting it up for growth that we still enjoy now through his reforms and subsequent reforms of governments which were elected beyond him. But those reforms are hard to comprehend in terms of how difficult they would've been to achieve and the transformation that they did have on the Australian economy.

Of course, Bob Hawke's great partner in undertaking these reforms was the Treasurer and the subsequent Prime Minister, Paul Keating, who worked alongside the Prime Minister. But, as has also been noted, these reforms were also backed strongly and supported by the coalition at the time. Indeed, the shadow Treasurer for much of the Hawke era was a great friend and mentor of mine, the late Jim Carlton. He often used to say to me, when I asked him about his time as shadow Treasurer: 'You know what? We backed all the Hawke economic reforms. I actually used to have members of my own party come up to me and say, "Why are you constantly supporting these reforms?"' They could have made great political mileage had they opposed those things such as floating the dollar or the tariff reductions or the like. Jim's response was very straightforward: they were good reforms, they were reforms that deserved to be backed and they were the right things to do. They were indeed very good reforms, and we still enjoy the legacy of them.

It wasn't just the economic reforms, though, that Bob Hawke was so well known for and led but, indeed, many social, environmental and other reforms as well. The biggest social reform was, perhaps, the introduction of Medicare—something which we still enjoy today and will be everlasting. He also introduced the HECS system into the tertiary scheme. That enabled more money to come into higher education and, through that, enabled more people to access tertiary education.

His environmental record is also incredibly impressive, as many members have noted. He was also a great campaigner for Indigenous rights, as the person who set aside Uluru for the Indigenous people. One of his first actions after being elected Prime Minister was to amend the Aboriginal land rights act to return Uluru and Kata Tjuta National Park to the Anangu people. There were many other great reforms, which have been mentioned by others in this chamber as well.

Bob Hawke was not just a great reformer; he was also an incredible individual. Again, this has been commented on by many people in this chamber. He was a larrikin, he was a person who enjoyed a beer and he loved his sport. He was a classic Australian in that regard, despite being an incredible academic and intellect in his own right. As the Prime Minister noted earlier today, he had a capacity to feel and not to disguise or hide his emotions. He shed tears and, at times, rose to anger. He expressed joy and he was empathic as well, but that maybe that was because he had his own share of pain. Indeed, we do know a share of that pain that he had, with his brother dying and his son dying at an early age. He had the tragedy associated with his own children as well. He did not hide that pain. He did not hide the authentic man that he was. I think that was one of the reasons that the Australian people did love him so much. It was for the reforms that he led but also the authenticity of the man he was.

He was an incredible Australian, and we are right to dedicate this day in his honour. I also join the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition and others in passing on my condolences to Blanche and his family. I thank them, along with thanking Hazel Hawke, for the contributions that they have made. All of us in this place know that we can't do our jobs here without the great support of the families that we have back home. Certainly, he had incredible support in Hazel Hawke during his prime ministership and incredible support in Blanche and the rest of his family as well. May Bob Hawke rest in peace.

Australian democracy distinguishes itself from other comparable democracies in a whole range of ways, but one of those ways is that Australians hold their leaders to a much higher standard in one key respect: we want them to be simultaneously extraordinary at the same time as we want them to be ordinary people like us. We truly want them to be a first among equals. Nobody in the history of this nation has passed this test more spectacularly and for longer than Bob Hawke, or been more admired for that than Bob Hawke.

As the 29th of the 63 speakers today, I'm conscious that so much has been said. There have been some terrific contributions and speeches. I want to single out my great mate, the member for Lingiari, for his speech earlier on today. They have been great speeches in tribute to Bob. While I don't agree with the member who was just up, who said that Bob's most important economic reforms were necessarily all supported by both sides of the parliament, as has been claimed again today, I don't want to dwell on that or quibble with that. I do think there has been genuine warmth from his former political adversaries. I'm thankful for that, and I wanted to acknowledge that.

Bob's passing on the Thursday before the election was such an emotional night for all of us who loved Bob Hawke and the country he served, and for anyone who still believes that national leadership can bring people together and lift them up in a common cause. The legacy that Bob left was unparalleled in peacetime. If you compare what he inherited in 1983 with what he bequeathed to the nation in 1991, the country at the end of that period was almost unrecognisable from what he had inherited. He had inherited a closed economy, a stagnant country and a divided society. By 1991, he had opened the economy up to the world, he had played a role in the end of apartheid and he had played a role in protecting Antarctica. There was the superannuation reform, there was Medicare and there were all of these important reforms that we treasure so much.

I was talking with the member for Brand earlier on, and we were saying that even one of those legacies on its own would be enough to sustain most of the people who come here. Thinking that you may have one of those legacies in your biography by the time you finish up as a member of parliament would make most people sleep pretty easily and pretty proudly at night. But the fact that Bob, working in partnership with the great Paul Keating, was able to deliver so many of those reforms, such a broad and long-lasting legacy, is evidence of the highest imaginable standard of political leadership in this country.

But beyond all the measurable things and all the tangible legacies that Bob left, there are the less tangible things as well: the unmeasurable difference that he made to the country—the change in the way we saw ourselves, the culture, the confidence and the bounce in the step that Bob gave us as our national leader. Whether Bob took on the best characteristics of this country or the country took on the best characteristics of Bob, he personified what we wanted and want Australia to be—self-assured, forward-looking, intelligent and fun as well, all at the same time.

One of the enjoyable things about today is hearing about his specific connections with ordinary communities right around Australia. My community is no different in having ways that we felt especially connected to Bob. One of those ways is through my predecessor, Craig Emerson. The former member for Rankin was such a dear friend of Bob's—more like a son of Bob's than a friend, even. As I understand it, Craig was there when Bob passed from this world. He played such a supportive role with the family. He loved Bob so much and he loved Bob's family so much that he played such a supportive role in the last days of Bob's life and, indeed, in organising that truly remarkable service, that memorial, that I was privileged to be a part of and attend in Sydney a few weeks ago. I want to recognise in this place Craig's relationship with Bob and the role that he played in delivering that commemoration in Sydney.

Bob also had a remarkable connection to our multicultural communities in my electorate. The first service in my part of the world recognising Bob's passing was actually at the Cambodian Buddhist temple in Marsden. They put on a service with speeches, commemorations and traditional Buddhist prayer. It was a remarkable way to celebrate Bob's life, and it was an indication for me of the different ways that he touched all of the communities that make up this country that we serve today.

The first memory of my life—and I'm not talking about my first political memory—the first thing I can remember in my entire life was the America's Cup win in 1983. I was five years old. My dad came and woke me up, and, if I'm remembering it correctly, it was before dawn. I think it was in September of 1983. My dad came and woke me up early and we watched Australia II cross the line and we saw that footage, which has been played almost on loop ever since Bob passed, of the remarkable jacket and the remarkable statement that he made when we won the America's Cup for the first time in all of those years. I remember being a primary school student having the nightly news at six o'clock dominated by two people—Bob Hawke, who made me proud to be an Australian, and Sir Joh Bjelke-Petersen, who didn't make me so proud to be a Queenslander. I think, if anything, that was probably the first stirrings of a Labor heart—seeing the comparison between Bob Hawke on our news and his role in our country and Sir Joh and his role in our state.

I remember borrowing the Hawke memoirs. As a high school student, I was the first the first person to borrow the Hawke memoirs from the Springwood library. I stayed up half the night for a week or so getting through the Hawke memoirs. But I didn't meet him until 2001. I met him in 2001 because I was a campaign volunteer on the Eden-Monaro campaign, not far from here, and I overheard a conversation between the campaign organisers saying that they didn't have anyone to pick Bob up from the airport. So I volunteered and I went and collected him. I took him to the Queanbeyan shops, and I had never seen anything like it at that point. I was a young PhD student at the ANU and when I took him to the Queanbeyan shops they mobbed him in ways that I had not seen before and probably haven't seen since. There was one woman who got him to sign the butcher's paper wrapped around a kilo of snags that she had just bought from the butcher and there was another mum who got him to sign the back of her receipt from Coles or Woollies, which she'd just come out of, just so that they could prove to their friends that they had met the great man when they were at the Queanbeyan shops that day.

The next year I spent proper time with Bob. I wrote for him, along with Tim Gartrell and Geoff Walsh, who I worked for at the time. We worked with Bob Hawke and Neville Wran on the 2002 review of the Australian Labor Party. I got to go around the country with Bob and also with Neville Wran. It was one of the treasured experiences of my professional life to spend so much time with those two giants, and also with Geoff and Tim working on that project. There are two stories that I remember above all about that period. The first was on the day that they were releasing the report. They were going to release it down at the Labor Party offices not far from here on National Circuit. My job was to go get Neville and drop him off at the national secretariat and then go get Bob and drop him off. I went to get Neville and, as we were getting near the Labor Party offices, we saw that there was a scrum of about 40 people from the media. Cameras, microphones and journalists were everywhere on the steps of the national secretariat. Neville said to me, 'Do you mind—just drop us around the back.' So I dropped him around the back; we got Nifty in through the back door. I went and got Bob then. We were getting near the national secretariat, and all the journos were still there. I said to Bob, and I thought I was being helpful, 'Bob, we'll just go around the back there and we'll go through the back door.' He said, 'Don't be crazy—drop me right in the middle of that mob!' The difference between Neville and Bob was that Bob was desperate to talk to those 40 journalists who'd assembled on the stairs.

The other thing was, on the same day, probably some hours later—the wee hours of that night or the following morning—after having released this report that we wrote with Hawke and Wran, a few of the staff were out and about in Canberra. I'd remembered reading in Blanche's first book about Bob that, when he was a PhD student at the ANU, one night there was some kind of celebration, and he swam the length of the fish pond at University House, which is a long fish pond but not very deep. I remembered this story at the least productive time, and so we decided we would all go swim the length of the fish pond at ANU in tribute to Bob. We did that, and that was not the wisest thing I've ever done. I can report to the House that today is a far more civilised way of paying tribute to Bob than how we paid tribute to him on that night in the freezing cold in 2002.

The last time I saw Bob was on his verandah. I note that a lot of my colleagues have had the same experience. It really does go to the generosity of Bob with his time that so many of us in this place have spent one-on-one time with Bob on the verandah of that spectacular Sydney home that he shared with Blanche. I've sat out there with him with the cigar, the crossword and the big pink milkshake. The thing that really struck me from the last time I did that, which was last year, was that I kept trying to learn from him things that he had gleaned when he was Prime Minister or things from his period when he was most active in public life. The really impressive thing though was that he only wanted to talk about the here and now or the future. He wanted to talk about where the economy was headed, where the jobs were going to come from and how Labor was going to be a party of the future and not a party of the past. It was really impressive in hindsight in particular—I was also impressed at the time, obviously. Now that I think about those few conversations that I did get to have with Bob, it was remarkable how often he was focused on what was next and not just on that remarkable legacy that he left.

I think it is true that Australia is not only not the same without him in his passing but also not the same after that remarkable contribution that he made. We pay tribute to him with our words today and we treasure forever the remarkable standard that he set and the legacy that he left all of us.

With Bob Hawke's passing, we've lost one of our nation's greatest—one of our greatest Prime Ministers and one of our greatest Australians. He was a fighter for a fair go for all, because he believed in us and he believed in an egalitarian Australia. He loved people, and they loved him. I confess: I loved him. I would not be here in this place as one of his successors as the MP for Wills if it weren't for him. He gave my migrant family equality of opportunity, access to affordable public housing, access to health care through Medicare and access to a good education. I and millions of Australians owe him a great debt of gratitude to go with our enduring love for him.

Like many of my colleagues, I caught up with Bob many times. I caught up with him a number of times over the past 12 years—more recently in his office but more memorably on that back verandah for a few beers. Knowing my work in foreign policy, he would often regale me with his stories of the work that he did and the achievements—whether it was with President Reagan, President Gorbachev, President Mandela or Prime Minister Rocard, the French Prime Minister. But when I asked him what he was most proud of, given all these great historic moments that he was part of, he thought about it and he told me that it was this: before his time as Prime Minister, only about 30 per cent of Australian kids graduated from high school, and, because of the policies he put in place as Prime Minister, after he left it was over 80 per cent. I was one of them. I graduated in 1990, a migrant kid from a housing commission. He was a legend, and it was a great honour to spend that time with him. I remember saying to him, on more than one occasion, 'Bob, the Australian people will never love a Prime Minister as much as they love you.' He really loved that, so much so that he invited me to a round of golf. I never got a chance to have that round of golf with him, but every time he saw me after that he gave me a big hug, which was fantastic.

I have the honour of representing the electorate of Wills, as Bob did. It has certainly changed a lot since Bob's time there, but the people of Wills still remember their former member and Prime Minister with great love and gratitude. Mimi Tamburrino, who worked for Bob in his electorate office for 12 years, told me about a time in early 1981, before Bob was Prime Minister, when he was just the regular member for Wills. He walked in straightaway and wanted to see her, and he said: 'Here, Mimi; here's 20 bucks. Go down to the TAB and put a bet each way on this horse—five bucks each way on this one and $5 each way on that one,' and so on. This was a very young Mimi, and she said, 'All right, but what is the TAB?' She had no idea. He was incredulous and he bellowed, 'What do you mean you don't know what a TAB is?' Years later, when Bob was Prime Minister, he'd walk in to his electorate office—on the occasion that he had the time—and he'd say: 'Mimi, what's been happening in the electorate office? Are you looking after the people?' She'd say, 'Yes, Bob, but the people want to talk to the member for Wills,' and he would respond, 'What do they want to see me for? You're the member for Wills; I'm just the Prime Minister.'

So I want to thank all the people of the electorate of Wills who have so generously shared their memories of Bob with me in recent weeks. I've had a condolence book out the front of the electorate office in Coburg, and people have left some really beautiful messages for Bob's family. I'll just read you a couple of those messages. From Julie: 'Bob, you made an everlasting impression on Australia. You brought the Labor Party together and showed Australia what a progressive and kind government can do. Thank you.' From Donna: 'One less light on the hill with your passing.'

He changed so many lives in our local electorate of Wills, but he changed the nation. He made better the lives of millions of Australians, with the economic reforms that we've heard about, modernising the Australian economy, the foundation of our decades of prosperity; with wonderful social policy, which I touched on and which affected my family—Medicare and education; and with environmental policy—saving Kakadu, the Daintree and the Franklin and working with Prime Minister Rocard, the French Prime Minister, on saving Antarctica. That equality of opportunity, which means so much to all of us, means that much of the Australia that we know today would not exist if it were not for Bob's golden years as Prime Minister.

And he changed the world for the better as well. He had a critical role in elevating Australia's place in the world and a critical role in defeating apartheid. Robert Ray, one of his very talented and formidable cabinet ministers, reminded me that, above all of this, there was this immense work ethic that Bob had, to make it all possible. He had the courage of his convictions. He did what was right even when it was hard. In 1988, Bob refused to buckle on what was then the populist push for Australia to limit Asian immigration. He reaffirmed Australia's commitment to an immigration system that did not discriminate based on race or ethnicity, and in the wake of the Tiananmen Square protests Bob offered asylum to Chinese students studying in Australia. That was the right thing to do. He had the conviction of his values, even if they were not popular at that particular time.

That is what true leaders do—they lead. This is Bob's legacy: leadership through conviction, public service through love. That is the legacy that I'll hold on to as I go about my day-to-day work for the people I represent in my electorate of Wills and here in this chamber for the national interest.

Wherever he's looking down upon us, or wherever he might be right now: Bob, we shed so many tears for you when we heard you passed. We will all remember your public service and your love for us and for our country, and we thank you for giving so much of yourself to all of us. I would not be here in this place if it were not for you, nor would millions of Australians have been given the opportunity to fulfil their potential if it were not for you. My condolences go out to the entire Hawke family and to Blanche, who told me at the memorial that it was now time to celebrate his remarkable life. I want to end with one very simple message from that condolence book left there: 'Bob, you gave so much to Australia and Wills. You'll be missed but never forgotten.' Vale, Bob Hawke. Rest in peace.

I was pretty scared the first time I phoned Robert James Lee Hawke. I was 19, it was 7:05 in the morning and my first words to him were something like: 'Hello, Mr Hawke. It's Susan from the Mike Carlton show. I'll put you on hold and Mike will be with you shortly.' With that, I flicked him to hold, and buzzed Mike Carlton and said, 'The Prime Minister is on line 3.' It was 1983. Yes, I was a switchboard girl for the 2GB breakfast show and, over the next two years, Bob Hawke was a not-infrequent interviewee of that gifted broadcaster. They were a good match, and I was always a little bit scared that I'd lose the phone call on its way to the studio.

This might have been my only personal contact with the Prime Minister, but in 1985 I found myself at much closer quarters, here in Canberra in the old parliament's press gallery as 2UE's cadet reporter. I don't think I was any less scared of the man we called Hawke in those early years, terrified that, in a media conference, I would ask a stupid question, particularly with the likes of Laurie Oakes, Paul Lyneham and Ian McMinn as witnesses—not to mention a few others who are still in the gallery today. But what I slowly understood as the years went on was that I was watching an extraordinary man in action, a man who could read an audience and, using language, intellect and performance, find a way to take them with him on a journey of change without them being gripped by fear.

He wasn't a bloke I watched on TV. As a radio journalist, I was usually standing just behind his shoulder, my arm wrapped around him with my microphone being supported. I had to make sure my microphone wasn't in range of the cameras, because the camera crew got stroppy about that. It was a real physical proximity, and it's one which few people encounter in their workplace. You do get to see people up really close. I got to study the back of his head and his ear at various close quarters as I recorded every word. I had a moment of horror last year when, along with two other former press gallery journalists, Anna Grutzner and Christine Wallace, I was talking to some politics students at ANU and realised we were probably considered primary historical sources for the Hawke years.

The member for Lingiari's comments really resonated with me earlier today, about the way that the caucus and that government worked and the incredible people who made up the team led by Bob Hawke. My recollection is that Hawke exuded energy. I saw someone gathering people to him to work through issues, to reform whole industries, like footwear and clothing, in the fairest way. I saw women being given roles. I saw warmth and respect for people from all walks of life. I saw fierce debates of ideas, which I recall as being much more issues driven than personality driven. And, as the member for Lingiari noted, I do recall how long some of those cabinet meetings went, because the media were all hanging around waiting for them to end—but at least in those days parliament had a non-members bar!

Bob's body language was rich and not always effectively captured by radio. On the days I covered question time, he would inevitably glance up at the press gallery—it was all smaller and closer than it is in here—and, before he delivered a mischievous line or a key remark, I swear you could see his eyes sparkle and a cheeky grin just at the edge of his mouth. At regular early-morning doorstops, where it wasn't pre-arranged as it is these days and we were just there in the hope that someone would say something, my reporter's heart would lift when his car pulled up at the front steps, because it meant I'd likely have a grab for radio that morning. I recall that, while he was often fantastic media talent, there were also media conferences where I'd take my tape back to the office and have to do a lot of rolling edits to edit out long pauses to get my 30-second grab.

So he was formative for me in my experiences of being a young journalist. I did my first overseas reporting trip with Hawke to New Zealand to meet Prime Minister David Lange for ANZUS discussions. The first time I flew on the VIP with him was to the special Commonwealth heads of government meetings in London in 1986 to consider the recommendations of the eminent persons group on economic sanctions against South Africa, where British Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher and he did not see eye to eye. In the 1987 election campaign, I jumped into a Chinook with him as the pool radio reporter to cover an announcement relating to his plan to see the Daintree receive World Heritage listing, which he achieved in 1988. He loomed large in my life, a big personality, and to see him on the international stage in later years, when I was a journalist in New York and London—his intelligence, his ability to have Australia stand tall alongside much bigger nations—was something that made you proud to be an Australian.

After nearly 20 years away from the political sphere, I became re-acquainted with Bob in 2010 when he generously agreed to launch my first campaign. He came to Windsor, not an area known for its Labor roots, and after the official proceedings, which included him singing Happy Birthday to me to mark that day, we took off down Windsor Mall. As other speakers have noted, it wasn't long before one recognised him and word went out. He was mobbed, and the love for him from people of all political persuasions was palpable. Having lost the 2010 election, I sought advice from Bob: any suggestions on what I should do differently? His response? 'Learn to drink beer.' As a non-beer drinker, I've struggled with that one, but fortunately I have wonderful cider producers in my electorate, and that's proved a useful and enjoyable substitute. I think he'd approve.

At the memorial at the Sydney Opera House, I was assured by several of Bob's staff who were with him in the old parliament in the eighties, when we were there, that he would have drawn on something he often said when talking about horseracing results to remark on my recent re-election to this place: 'Doesn't matter if you win by a nose or a length; the bookies still pay out.'

Like so many people whose lives he touched, I extend my condolences to Blanche and his children and grandchildren, and I'm pleased that we can have the opportunity to express how grateful we are to have been gifted with his wisdom, his love of life, his love of Labor and his love of Australia.

It's with great sadness but also with great honour that I rise to pay tribute to an outstanding Australian—the 23rd Prime Minister, Bob Hawke—and to celebrate his remarkable life and leadership. We all felt such a deep sense of loss in hearing that Bob had passed away on 16 May this year. In fact, that deep sense of loss was felt right across the nation, across the political divide, across families and across communities. We also felt immense pride for his great achievements and his lifetime commitment to improving our nation. Bob Hawke truly embodied what it is to be a great Australian and a great Labor man, and the fact is he so significantly changed our nation for the better, with so many of those positive changes still resonating today. So to Bob's wife, Blanche, and to all of Bob's family, we send our deepest condolences at this time and we give thanks for Bob Hawke and his wonderful legacy.

Bob was a reformer through and through. He not only changed the Labor Party, he fundamentally changed Australia for the better. As Prime Minister from 1983 to 1991, Bob Hawke and his government brought in so many major economic and environmental reforms that endure to this very day. Of course, perhaps the most significant social reform implemented by his government was the introduction of Medicare, Australia's first affordable, universal system of health insurance, which fundamentally improved the lives and health outcomes of millions of people right throughout this country. Bob's environmental vision was also central to his wonderful legacy. Through his leadership, his government enacted legislation which gave the Commonwealth power over World Heritage sites, thus enabling the World Heritage listing of sites such as Kakadu. And, of course, it was Bob Hawke who stopped the damming of the Franklin River; banned uranium mining in Jabiluka; and created the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act, a great environmental legacy.

Bob also proved that you can have a country that treats and respects working men and women with fairness and at the same time you can have an economy that's open to the world. Bob Hawke brought the nation's powerful unions and big employers together in creating the wage accord. In doing this, he achieved that unprecedented level of harmony in industrial relations.

Bob Hawke was praised for modernising the national economy and integrating it into a global one. It was one of his government's first acts in 1983, in which he floated the Australian dollar, and this move was one of the first in a long line of deregulation undertaken by his government. Bob Hawke paved the way for foreign owned banks and institutions to trade and operate in Australia, and, of course, he also reduced tariffs. Other economic reforms included the founding of the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation forum, or APEC, established to promote participation and growth in our region.

Not only did Bob Hawke address inequality in our economy and open it up to the global market; he was at the forefront of creating a better, fairer and more inclusive society here at home. The Hawke government instituted some of the strongest protections against discrimination, which still stand today. But, of course, the fight for equality did not end there. Bob knew that improving the educational outcomes of all Australians would lead to a better life for future generations and it would also strengthen our economy—things like setting a national curriculum standard for schools, establishing national training and qualifications standards and upgrading the TAFE sector, together with increased funding, all played a vital role in improving educational outcomes for our nation. So very importantly, from his time in office the percentage of students in secondary school grew from 35 per cent to 77 per cent—a remarkable achievement. Student numbers in training and vocational education rose by over 25 per cent, and university enrolments rose by almost 57 per cent. The fact is that Bob Hawke's reforms to education ensured hundreds of thousands of Australians could access the life-changing benefits of furthering their education—another remarkable legacy.

All of his reforms, driven by his very core values of a fair go for all and focusing on those important matters like education, health and the environment, epitomise the Prime Minister and the man Bob Hawke. His government advanced economic reform, progressed social reform, and, of course, he is regarded as an absolute champion for protecting our natural environment. The fact is: Bob Hawke brought the Labor Party and the Australian public into the modern age. In typical 'Hawky' style, he did it by consensus and took us all along for that journey because he understood our greatest strengths flow from unity.

And Hawke was, of course, the most popular Prime Minister since the introduction of public opinion polls, and he used that popularity to create long-lasting change. To quote the great man himself, he said:

The essence of power is the knowledge that what you do is going to have an effect, not just an immediate but perhaps a lifelong effect, on the happiness and wellbeing of millions of people and so I think the essence of power is to be conscious of what it can mean for others.

Bob Hawke certainly had a lifelong effect—indeed, a generational effect—which has changed the lives of millions of Australians for the better. Ultimately, Bob Hawke left Australia a much fairer and much better place than he found it. Thank you, Bob Hawke, and farewell.

I join so many in this place, across both sides of the chamber, in paying my respects to the memory of the Hon. Robert James Lee Hawke AC and acknowledge the tremendous outpouring of both grief and joy that we experienced at the Sydney Opera House at his memorial service. In many ways, Bob Hawke's political life, and particularly his prime ministership and his time leading this country, reflects so many of the significant moments in my own life. Indeed, the 1983 election, when he was elected, was my first federal election to vote in. I'd just missed the 1980 election by a month. I'm sure my vote would have made a huge difference to Labor's cause at the 1980 election! However, I was very pleased that my first election, being the '83 election, saw the Hawke government take federal power and, more importantly, bring in a really important agenda that was about taking our nation to its potential. I think that was reflected not only in the economic, the international and the social areas but also in a really important conversation we had as a nation about what our future was. To be able to do that requires the leadership that so many people in this place have expressed, and trust, because they know that the leader understands them and that he epitomises their hopes. I think that's very much what Bob Hawke did. It was the case that so many of those changes were not easy. Looking back, it is easy to say that it was obvious and that they were the sorts of reforms that needed to happen, but at the same time they were not easy reforms, both within our own party and movement and within the national debate. It was certainly the case for me, at that point still a university student and newly married. It was tremendous to see a Labor government take that federal election.

Of course, he was re-elected in '84 and, by then, I had my first son, and then he was re-elected in 1990, when I had my second son, so his prime ministership encompassed a very significant part of my adult life. I have to say that, when I was born, I'm pretty certain no Bible fell open at any significant passage, so, when I voted in March 1983 as a uni student and young adult, I little imagined that I would have the great honour, through my own community, of standing in this parliament today acknowledging the enormous contribution that Bob Hawke made to this nation.

It is the case that Bob's government made significant and massive reforms that established this country for a long-term future of prosperity and fairness. Those two things, which my colleagues have talked about, have been profoundly important for all of us as we, as legislators, deal with the issues that face us. They have been well canvassed. Recognising that so many of my colleagues want to speak in this debate, I just want to use the opportunity to acknowledge the legacy of, in particular—as my colleague the member for Richmond talked about—education, which I am very passionate about. I want to use the opportunity to put on the record the great love and affection that my own region had for Bob and the great commitment that he gave us consistently. As a trade union leader, he was very conscious of a region like mine and was very supportive of us as we have, in particular, mining and steel industries, in which he was significantly involved. But also, as Prime Minister, his great engagement and love of the Illawarra was very obvious.

Like for so many in this place, Bob Hawke came and campaigned with me. It's hard to believe, when you listen to all the contributions, that there was only one man. It's hard to believe that there was a campaign by any of my colleagues in this place—probably only on this side of the House, to be fair—that Bob wasn't involved in. It's just an extraordinary thing. Our former leaders very often continue to support their party in that way, but the extent of what Bob did is just an extraordinary reflection of the fact he was Labor to his bootstraps. His love of the labour movement was profound. Whenever there was a call, right up to his very latest years, he was out there campaigning.

Certainly for me in 2004 and the by-election in 2002, he came and he did—as everyone has put on the record—the great walk through the shopping centre, getting mobbed by people, taking the time to shake hands, to talk to everybody who wanted to talk to him, do photos and all of those sorts of things, because Bob loved being amongst the people and he loved being there promoting Labor values and Labor candidates. He certainly did that for me. He did laugh and he would often say—because I was always quite overwhelmed and appreciative that a former Prime Minister came to help me along—'Of course a Hawke would come and help a Bird.' He had that slightly dad joke with me on all those occasions.

We really appreciated the support that he gave, but the community just reflected what so many have described as that great love affair that Australia had with Bob. Even years after he left parliament people would flock to talk to him. I just really want to record my personal appreciation for that. But in his great love for the Labor movement he also did that with the rank-and-file members. Members across both sides of the House would know about those dedicated volunteers. People like to be cynical about politics, but when you meet the rank-and-file members of the parties who are dedicated to the values, dreams and aspirations that you're trying to pursue and don't get anything out of it other than what they hope is something better for their community and their family, they're really amazing people.

Bob did the same for them in my area as well. I commented to my local media that I reckon there wouldn't be a Labor party member in my area who didn't have a photo with Bob Hawke, because whenever he was there he took the time to thank them for their support and efforts on behalf of the labour movement. I would like to use the last little bit of time to share some of their reflections, because I feel that this moment is as much theirs as it is mine as their representative. Chris Lacey is the president of the Thirroul branch. The Thirroul branch had its hundredth-anniversary dinner, and Bob Hawke came down and was the guest speaker. We had the obligatory singing of 'Solidarity Forever' but he didn't just pop in, do a speech, sing a song, thank everybody and head back to Sydney; he stayed, signed books, shook hands and took photos. Chris says:

Throughout his long life, Bob Hawke always had strong connections to Illawarra workers. His government saved the steel industry and played an important role in shaping modern multicultural Australia. In Thirroul we remember his involvement in our centenary celebration as a Labor branch and his memorably leading a rousing rendition of Solidarity forever.

Gino Mandarino, who is secretary of the Wollongong branch, said:

I first met him 32 years ago and numerous times over those decades. Bob inspired my interest in politics and public policy. He exemplified Bagehot's view of great prime ministers as men of commonplace opinions and uncommon administrative abilities. Bob Hawke was the best of the best, and like Whitlam and Wran, Labor leaders of the modern era, he now joins them of belonging to the ages.

Leighanne Hunt, who is secretary of the Port Kembla branch, said that Bob Hawke is the very definition of a conviction leader. Jess Malcolm-Roberts, secretary of Balgownie branch, said:

Bob Hawke didn't beat around the bush or pretend he was something he wasn't. Even though people might not have agreed with his position, they respected him for being genuine and it is this that Australians value the most. His policies shaped Australia into what it is today, bringing in many of the essential reforms my generation takes for granted. The Labor movement and all Australians are fortunate for having had him as our PM.

Dr Rowena Ivers, of the Thirroul branch, said, 'Thanks to Bob for Medicare—the gift that keeps on giving.'

I extend, as have so many in this place, my deepest condolences to Blanche, to all Bob's family and to his very many millions of friends around the country, so many of whom live in the Illawarra, and also pay respect to the great partnership he had with Hazel Hawke in that enormous period of leadership for our nation. I recognise Hazel's role in that. My deepest condolences: we are all so much better off for the life of Bob Hawke.

I'm pleased to follow the member for Cunningham in speaking to this condolence motion honouring the life of Robert James Lee Hawke, Australia's 23rd Prime Minister and Labor's longest-serving Prime Minister, who passed away on 16 May this year aged 89 years. I extend my sincere condolences to all of Bob's family members for they have lost the most and gave up so much of their time through Bob's public life. But he did his family and his country proud, and he will forever be one of Australia's favourite sons.

Bob entered public life as ACTU president in 1969. He was elected to federal parliament as the member for Wills in 1980. He became Labor's leader and Prime Minister in 1983, serving as Prime Minister until December 1991, and ended his parliamentary career in 1992. That was just over 12 years of service as a parliamentarian, When I think back at what he achieved in those 12 years, it is indeed extraordinary.

I note in particular that Bob was the only Prime Minister born in South Australia—in Bordertown. Whilst he didn't enter parliament as a South Australian member, there were rumours that he was looking at the safe Labor seat of Bonython in the late 1970s, with the backing of the very influential Mick Young. The seat was subsequently taken by the then Senate hopeful Neal Blewett, who, rumour had it, switched from his Senate aspirations in order to prevent Bob from taking that seat. The irony in that story is that Neal went on to serve in the Hawke government as minister for health and oversaw the reintroduction of the rebadged universal health care we now know as Medicare, for which Bob is perhaps best remembered, and which the coalition pledged to do away with at that time and at each of the subsequent elections of 1984, 1987, 1990 and 1993. After four failed attempts, I suspect that John Howard realised the foolishness of persisting with the attack on Medicare.

Since his passing, much has been said about Bob Hawke's political legacy and there is widespread agreement that the governments he led between 1983 and 1991 served Australia well and introduced groundbreaking reforms that were in the national interest and continue to be so. It would be fair to say, however, that he also led a very competent team of ministers. And because Labor had not been in government for long in the post-World War II years, there was so much to do. Hawke's government got on with the job of transforming the economy, changing environmental laws, changing industrial relations in this country, and increasing and improving the health services of all Australians. Others have spoken at length about his intervention in Indigenous affairs, and we even had a new national anthem introduced under his leadership.

Bob's strength, however, was his ability to bring Australians together. He knew that, if everyone was on board, more could be achieved and everyone would prosper. It's a rare characteristic that I have not seen matched by any other national leader since. Bob had good political judgement and a good political mind. But it was his ability to connect with every sector of society, and to win over their trust, that made him successful and respected. People felt comfortable with Bob Hawke. He was one of us, regardless of who he mixed with.

It was a unique personality trait that I saw for myself when Bob Hawke joined me on the campaign trail in Makin in my first two election campaigns. We just heard the member for Cunningham talking about her experiences with Bob, and I can say it was exactly the same when he came to Makin. It was as if he knew the place and it was his own home turf. We visited workplaces, community centres and shopping centres. Wherever we went, Bob was completely at home and people felt equally comfortable approaching and speaking to him. They wanted to meet him. He inspired people, and they trusted him. He was still incredibly sharp and had not lost any of his political wit, even though it had been years since he had been Prime Minister of this country, nor had he lost his confidence or his skill in responding to the many media inquiries. To this day, I still cross paths with people from Makin who fondly recall Bob's visit there. I will personally cherish my own memories of our campaign time together, and I thank him for his support in my own journey to this place.

The general public perception of politicians today is not good, yet in the days following his death, when people were frequently commenting about Bob Hawke, I didn't hear a single detrimental comment about him. That in itself is unique—that no-one had anything bad to say about him, and I spoke to a cross-section of people throughout my electorate. Regrettably, it is often only when a person dies that we take the time to reflect on what we have lost and the contribution that person made to our own life and to the lives of others. So, as we all reflect on the life of Bob Hawke and turn our minds to his contribution in public office, it becomes more clear than ever before that our nation is a better place because of him, that he was one of a kind and that we could all learn from him.

As important as they all were, it was not, however, Bob's government's achievements that he will be remembered best for but his persona: the beer-drinking, sport-loving larrikin who was just as much at home at an Aussie barbecue or in the front bar of the pub as he was in the mansions of millionaires or on the international stage with the leaders of the world. He endeared himself to the Australian people to an extent that few others have, and, not surprisingly, thousands of people attended or watched telecasts of his funeral service. Bob Hawke, the people's Prime Minister, has passed away, but his legacy will endure, and he will be remembered affectionately for decades to come. Vale Bob Hawke.

As many other speakers have said, Bob Hawke was a great Australian. He understood the Australian people, and the Australian people loved him for that. He was one of us. He could relate to anyone. But, as well as being a fine Australian and one of us, he was a great internationalist. He was a confident and respected advocate for Australia and championed Australia being an influential middle power in international forums. One of his earliest motivations for joining the Labor club at UWA, the University of Western Australia, was his interest in Labor's and Arthur Calwell's immigration policies. This led him to form an international club at the uni. He also saw the immigration policy as good economic policy as well as being good values based humanitarian policy.

Bob's father, Clem, was a Congregational minister, and Bob loved Clem. Often—and in my case it's the same—we take our religious beliefs, our values, from our parents, and I'm sure that that was the case with Bob. He was active in the student Christian movement and the Congregational Youth Fellowship. I understand from my research that Bob was actually impressed with some aspects of the Catholic faith, but he never pursued that. But he did head off to India for the World Conference of Christian Youth, and he was shocked by the huge gap between rich and poor and also the apparent indifference of some of the conference delegates to those people in dire poverty around them.

As a Rhodes scholar at Oxford he met bright young people from all around the world. I congratulate the government on establishing the Bob Hawke scholarship for the education of bright young Australians at fine overseas institutions. I think it's important.

Back in Australia, Bob at the ACTU and then through the International Labour Organization quickly became involved in international issues, becoming a strong supporter of Israel. Bob advocated for Russian Jews who were being denied permission to emigrate to Israel and led a strong union campaign of opposition to the South African Springboks tour of Australia. This was a significant shift in international opposition to apartheid in South Africa, which Bob continued as Prime Minister, and which led to mutual respect and friendship with Nelson Mandela. Bob was solid on the US alliance. Western Australian Governor Kim Beazley pointed me to Bob's autobiography where, in part, he said:

I was a vocal opponent of the war and of US policy in Vietnam but my attitude towards America remained positive. To me, the overriding importance of Australia's US alliance was clear. The United States, whatever its mistakes, was the bulwark of the free world.

Bob also foresaw the rise of Asia and of China and he understood that coming rise better than most. After the massacre at Tiananmen Square Bob, as Prime Minister, invited Chinese students in Australia to stay on if they wanted to. These people, these Chinese citizens who stayed in Australia proved to be an enormous asset to Australia. Bob's act of leadership demonstrated his commitment to a multicultural Australia. I won't go into them much, but we've heard much about the reforms of the education sector that meant more high school graduates and more university graduates.

The Hawke government's reform of the Australian economy, particularly the floating of the dollar, transformed us into a globally competitive trading nation. Bob was a great Prime Minister and a great Australian leader, as demonstrated by his actions both at home and abroad. He made us a more confident and outward looking nation. My earliest memory of Bob was when we won the America's Cup in 1983. I had just turned 12 years old. I remember it being early morning, and we were watching the victorious crew of Australia II and our Prime Minister. I remember bounding out of our house to catch the school bus, and I was so proud. I can remember my heart being so full of pride, seeing that crew and our Prime Minister. I remember thinking, 'Australians can do anything,' and I remember thinking to myself by extension as a young lad growing up in a country town—not in the city, not from a privileged family—that I could do anything. That was the feeling he gave me. I'll never forget how that made me feel.

I want to acknowledge Bob's love of the Territory as well. I've received many messages from people in Darwin and Palmerston, my electorate, and from Territorians elsewhere about how much they loved Bob. We've heard something from my friend and fellow Territorian the member for Lingiari and others about the handing back of Uluru to its traditional owners and how important that was, about the extension of the protections of Kakadu and, of course, about when Bob received the Barunga Statement.

My friend Father Frank Brennan reminded me today that it was in August 1988 in this place that Prime Minister Bob Hawke proposed that the first item of substantive business in this new Parliament House would be around reconciliation with First Nations people. The idea for the motion arose when the heads of Australian churches issued a statement titled 'Towards reconciliation in Australian society' at the beginning of the year of the bicentenary. For the record, this was the motion:

That this House—

(1) acknowledges that:

  (a) Australia was occupied by Aborigines and Torres Strait Islanders who had settled for thousands of years before British settlement at Sydney Cove on 26 January 1788;

  (b) Aborigines and Torres Strait Islanders suffered dispossession and dispersal upon acquisition of their traditional lands by the British Crown; and

  (c) Aborigines and Torres Strait Islanders were denied full citizenship rights of the Commonwealth of Australia prior to the 1967 Referendum;

(2) affirms:

  (a) the importance of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander culture and heritage; and

  (b) the entitlement of Aborigines and Torres Strait Islanders to self-management and self-determination subject to the Constitution and the laws of the Commonwealth of Australia; and

(3) considers it desirable that the Commonwealth further promote reconciliation with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander citizens providing recognition of their special place in the Commonwealth of Australia.

Bob remained committed, and I saw that commitment just in recent times in the Northern Territory. The Barunga Statement always stayed with him. In fact, he gave his resignation speech from the prime ministership in front of the Barunga Statement in this building.

My most recent memory of Bob was a simple interaction when we travelled to Arnhem Land for the Garma Festival, hosted by the Yolngu people, where he was continuing to promote reconciliation. We were going through the airport in Nhulunbuy—or Gove as some people might know the town—in Arnhem Land, and when he went through the security screening Bob was pulled up by security for having two cigarette lighters in his pocket. Only one cigarette lighter was allowed per person and he was asked by security to surrender one. Bob didn't make too much fuss about this, but I could see that he was slightly frustrated. I leaned in and said, 'Bob, I'd be honoured to receive one of your lighters.' He simply turned to me, smiled and said, 'Thanks mate.' And I said, 'No, thank you, Bob.' He smiled with that twinkle in his eye and I was a 12-year-old kid again. It was like when I bounded out of our house after seeing our Prime Minister with the America's Cup. Bob looked at me and said, 'Thanks mate,' and I just felt really special.

And I guess that was Bob's wonderful gift to Australians—he made all of us feel so special and so proud. Vale, Bob Hawke. My condolences to all who loved him so much.

'And his ghost may be heard as you pass by that billabong.' Bob's voice echoed out across the room as he remembered every verse of 'Waltzing Matilda'. We were all captured by him and his incredible voice. It was 2014 and he was, as usual, singing 'Waltzing Matilda' at the Jack Newton Celebrity Classic golf tournament. He was the patron of what has now become known affectionately as The Jack. In 1983, when Bob Hawke became Prime Minister, Jack Newton asked Bob to become the patron of The Jack celebrity golf tournament. Bob relished that job. He turned up almost every year, and almost every year he sang 'Waltzing Matilda' at the dinner. We just loved him doing it. He understood Australians and Australia. He loved us and we loved him.

I didn't know Bob particularly well or for very long. In fact, I only met him for the first time in 2014. It was when I was playing golf up in the Hunter, and when Bob made the annual trek up to The Jack. That evening I received a phone call from one of the organisers of the tournament to say that Mr Hawke had noticed me out on the golf course and wondered if I'd like to have breakfast with him the next morning and have a chat about golf, because he'd learnt that I was a keen golfer and had known Jack Newton for a number of years, and his lovely wife, Jackie, as well.

I jumped at that opportunity and fronted up, bright and early, the next morning to have breakfast with Bob. It was just the two of us and an incredible moment in my life. We chatted about many things, with much of the conversation being around golf. We talked about politics, we talked about Australia and we talked about my love of economics as a schoolgirl. In fact, in 1983 in year 7, Mr Kemp, my teacher, had meticulously instilled in us the lessons of the accord, the deregulation of the banking industry, the floating of the Australian dollar, the opening up of Australia's economy. That instilled in me a great love of economics and, of course, Bob Hawke.

I went on to study economics, including three-unit economics, right through school and my HSC. I always had the great pairing of Hawke and Keating in my mind as I trawled through those essays. I told Bob about that. He was mightily chuffed that my economics teacher had really deified Bob in my mind as a schoolgirl. We also talked about the influence of China on him and, importantly, Asia generally. He really understood our country and our place in the world and he spoke to me about how important it would be for us to continue with that engagement of Asia, while still recognising the importance of the alliance with the Americans.

After we'd chatted for probably half an hour or 40 minutes, we both knew that we had to finish our breakfast and go and tee off. He said to me, 'Meryl, have you ever thought about politics?' I said, 'Actually, Bob, I'd like to do that one day. But I've got a family and I'm working in the media, working on radio, and I love radio.' He said, 'You know what? Politics needs good strong women like you, good communicators. You really should give it some thought.' So when the opportunity came up in 2016 for me to run for the seat of Paterson I had those words of Bob Hawke ringing in my head. I thought, 'Maybe I should just listen to this knowledgeable and great man. Maybe I should just take up the challenge of politics.' So in his own way, he had a direct influence on me being here in this place today, and for that I will be eternally grateful.

May Bob's ghost be heard around this place as we go about governing this great nation of ours. May we continue to grow his legacy. Vale, the great Robert James Lee Hawke AC.

I thank the honourable member for Paterson. The question is that the motion be agreed to. I call the honourable member for Dunkley and I congratulate her on her admission to our parliament. I wish her all the very best.

Ms Murphy:

Thank you very much, Mr Deputy Speaker.
You're welcome.

Ms Murphy:

Early one morning in September 1983, a sports-loving and somewhat argumentative nine-year-old girl was utterly convinced that her new Prime Minister had just given her an iron-clad argument to stay home from school that day. I said, 'Hey, Mum, you know how you always say you're the boss of me? Well, if you try to make me go to school today you're a bum.' It turns out that line worked so well for Bob Hawke but not so much for me. Off to school I went.

He didn't get me out of a day of learning, but Bob Hawke, with his now infamous response to our victory in the America's Cup, had given me my first political memory and one of my first debating defeats. Many more with my mother were to come. And he gave me and very many other Australians, and not to mention people from across our region and the world, so much more. My generation, whose formative teenage years were that remarkable and somewhat maligned decade of the 1980s, had the privilege of growing up while Bob Hawke; his Treasurer, Paul Keating; and his very talented government transformed our country. It did not matter that, for most of us, the closest we ever got to Bob Hawke was the nightly TV news. His presence, his personality and the pure Bob Hawkeness of him meant that we knew that he was our Prime Minister not just the Prime Minister.

That charismatic presence, which so many people have spoken of in this place today, was undoubtedly one of Bob Hawke's greatest gifts. But, in my humble opinion, having that great gift was not the most important thing about him. In my view, it was what he harnessed that great gift to achieve that was most important and who and what he harnessed it for. Of course, he harnessed it for the transformational policy reforms which, as we all know, spanned the economic, the environmental and the social policy realms and which are on both the domestic and the international stage.

There was also something else that he gave us which I value to this very day: Bob Hawke demonstrated to Australians that our federal government could and should be compassionate and expansive. He demonstrated that it could not just talk about equality but fight for it, particularly for those in our community who don't have the power or the resources to fight for it on their own. He demonstrated that it could take everyone on a journey of bold and progressive change that was based on a love of Australia and Australians and an unwavering commitment to equality and justice. For some of us who grew up in the magnificent eighties and whose conscious experience of politics and government was dominated by Bob Hawke, he gave us a lasting, enduring belief that that is what a federal government always should be and do. For a lot of us, he gave us a beating Labor heart, and we will forever be grateful.

The first Labor member for Dunkley, the wonderful Bob Chynoweth, was first elected to the Australian parliament in the 1983 Hawke slide. I know Bob would have loved to have been able to speak today. I'm pretty sure he would have told this story better than me, but I will do my best. Newly preselected to a seat that we had lost in a by-election mere months earlier and thrown into a snap election, Bob Chynoweth received a visit from the great man early on in the campaign. Hawkie inquired, 'How are you doing, Bob?' 'I'm just really hanging on to your coat-tails, to tell you the truth,' the ever-humble Chynoweth confessed. Bob's response was: 'Don't worry, mate. So is everyone else.' I'm so glad, and I'm sure we're all so glad, that everyone was.

We have heard a lot today about how the Hawke government and Hawke personally protected Antarctica. It was the wonderful Bob Chynoweth from my electorate of Dunkley who, years after he was first elected, went on a parliamentary delegation to Antarctica and had a personal epiphany. In his words, he sat there and said: 'We have magnificent places like this. We must protect them.' Bob, when he returned back to our community and then to Canberra, started a campaign to influence the Hawke government and its cabinet ministers to reverse Australia's position, as it was then, on the mineral convention. Bob Chynoweth lobbied cabinet ministers, but he also collected more than 20,000 signatures of Australian citizens and tabled them in this place, asking for Australia's position to change.

When that went before the Hawke cabinet, as we all know, Hawke took up that challenge. He took up that challenge and he fought for something that was right, something that was lasting and something that was a legacy. My community are very proud of the role that our former Labor member for Dunkley played in that legacy. But it's not just a legacy of great policy; it is a demonstration, an illustration, of what Bob Hawke taught people of my generation, and some of our parents, to expect from their federal government—that is, to identify what is important and what is right, and then to fight for it, even if fighting for it is hard; even if vested interests and the rules of the game say that you shouldn't; even if you have to battle to take others, including the electorate, with you.

Bob Hawke was Australia's greatest Prime Minister. And while the generations that came after me didn't get the opportunity to live under his government, to this day they are no doubt continuing to benefit from the reforms that were put in place, and so will the generations to come. The great Bob Hawke will always be loved, and he will be missed. His legacy will live on. It will live on in the way he changed our country, and in the way he influenced the political philosophy of so many Australians—Australians that I am proud to say are like me. On behalf of my community of Dunkley, and on behalf of Bob Chynoweth, condolences to Bob Hawke's family and to all who loved him.

At the outset, may I pay tribute to the member for Dunkley on a terrific first, but not inaugural, speech. It was a great tribute to Bob Hawke.

He drinks like a fish, swears like a trooper, works like a demon, performs like a playboy, talks like a truckie—and acts like a politician. Almost your cliche Aussie. Except in this case it's Bob Hawke, and the only typical thing about him is the way he's larger than average in almost everything. Bob Hawke is your typical Australian, oversize.

If you want to understand Australia's 23rd Prime Minister, you can do no better than to read Craig McGregor's 1977 profile. It's a time when Bob Hawke isn't Prime Minister, he's not even in parliament—he's running the ACTU—but it's an extraordinary insight into the man.

Craig McGregor follows Hawke for a few days, and in part of it he tells the story of Bob popping into a bar in Melbourne for a quick five-minute chat. Two hours and half-a-dozen drinks later, Hawke starts to leave. As McGregor recounts:

… he is the focus of a great deal of affection right across the spectrum: men, women, workers, sportsmen, businessmen. It buoys him up, even exhilarates him, but he remains easygoing, serious, arguing or defending himself when necessary.

McGregor tells the tale of Ron Barassi and Hawke running into one another in Melbourne and how:

… an elegantly dressed woman in huge, fashionable sunglasses … throws her arms around—

Hawke's neck—

and gives him a warm, sensuous kiss.

Hawke replies:

… "There's some bloody hope for Australia when women prefer politicians to footballers!"

And McGregor concludes with a description of a Hawke press conference. He says:

… a press conference by Hawke isn't just a conference, it's a media extravaganza, a glamourfest, a promotional wonder like those MGM spectaculars of old, in which Hawke plays King to his adoring court, fences and jibes with his carping Lords, puts down the media Fool, works himself into a lather of anger at The Enemy Outside, and finally sends his audience home, as after an Aristotelian tragedy, exhausted, admiring, and experiencing the exquisite pleasure of catharsis.

In his 3,207 days as Prime Minister, Bob Hawke saw Australia through an accord, ACT self-government, saving the Franklin, banning uranium mining in Jabiluka, handing back Uluru to its traditional owners, establishing APEC, an economic summit and floating the dollar. And there were many reforms that were not supported by the other side of the House: the Sex Discrimination Act, Medicare, capital gains taxation, fringe benefits taxation and asset-testing the pension. But the one reform I want to focus on today was his opening up of the Australian economy by bringing down the tariff walls.

When I was at university I wrote, in 1994, an honour's thesis on trade liberalisation and the Australian Labor Party. I was puzzled as to why Australia brought down our tariff walls not because other countries were going to do it as part of a deal, but unilaterally. And why in other countries it had been conservatives that brought down the tariff walls, whereas in Australia it was a party of the Left.
